text: AMADAN. See HAMADAN.

AMADI, a town of Western Africa, in the Mandingo territory, in N lat. 12° 20'.—Also a settlement on the N side of the Gambia, in N lat. 13° 2'. AMADIA, AMADIEH, or AMADIYAH, a sanjak and town in Turkish Kurdistan, on the Kurnab river. The town is 72 m. N of Mosul, in N lat. 36° 47′ 29" [Ainsworth], at an elevation of 4,265 ft. It is a place of some trade, and had, some years ago, a pop. of about 4,000. The vale of A. extends from the Tigris to the vale of Rowandiz, and is about 5 or 6 m. in width. The town is built on a terrace of limestone, and is surrounded by cliffs from 40 to 80 ft. in alt. The Jews have 70 houses, the Mahommedans 60, and the Chaldeans 20 here; but the town has been nearly desolated by recent events. [Geog. Journ., vol. xi.] The district forms a kind of independent Kurd principality, being governed by an hereditary prince, a descendant of the royal house of Abbas.
AMADINGEN, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Danube, on the Aach river. Pop. 900.
AMAFA, a town of Japan, on the E coast of the island of Nifon, 84 m. E of Jedo, in N lat. 35° 25'.
AMAFU, a town of Ashantee, 10 m. S of Coomassie, in N lat. 6° 41'.
AMAHOY, a town on the S coast of the island of Ceram, in S lat. 3° 20'.

AMAK, or AMAGER, an island of Denmark, on the northern skirts of which a part of the city of Copenhagen, with the suburb of Christianhaven, is built. It is about 4 m. long, and 2 m. broad, and contains several villages. It is separated by the Strömmer, a narrow channel, from the isle of Zealand, on which the greater part of Copenhagen is built, and with which it is connected by two bridges. It is principally laid out in gardens, and pasture and pleasuregrounds, forming in fact "one immense garden, from which all the vegetables, and the greater part of the fruit consumed in the city, are brought." [Inglis.]

AMAKOSA, a powerful tribe of Caffres in S Africa, whose territories immediately adjoin the British settlements on the N, and whose chief resided on the left or E bank of the Great Key river, before the limits of the British territory were advanced to the line of that river. The Bashee river formed the north-eastern limit of the Amakosa territory; and the Amatembu tribes, or Tambookies, inhabit the country N and W of the Amakosa. Steedman estimates their number at 150,000, of whom 25,000 are adult males. Lichtenstein estimated them at 30,000, and their territory at 16,200 sq. m., of 60 to a degree. Thompson estimates their territory at 14,000 sq. m.; and Cooley at 15,000. They are of indolent habits, leaving the cultivation of their land entirely to the women, while they employ themselves in the chase, or in herding cattle, which constitute their sole wealth. They smear their bodies with a composition of red clay and grease, and their short woolly hair is rolled into small round knots by a profuse application of the same ointment. When Sir Benj. D'Urban crossed the Key river in the Caffre war of 1835, he found a numerous pop. of Fingoes or wanderers of all tribes, living in a state of abject slavery under the Amakosa Caffres. They were offered land between the Lower Keiskamma and the Great Fish river, and were brought safely into their new location: a transaction constituting one of the most remarkable instances in history of the instant emancipation of a multitude of human beings from a state of abject bondage to freedom and comparative comfort. In the Caffre war of 1846, the Amakosa were driven beyond the territory bounded on the E by the Chumie river, from its rise in Gaika's Kop to its junction with the Keiskamma, and thence along the Keiskamma to its mouth. The territory thus acquired is to be occupied by a mixed population of Hottentots, Mozambiques, Caffres, and Fingoes.

AMAKOUA, a warlike and numerous nation of Eastern Africa, whose country extends from the Angozha river to Cape Delgado, and about 60 leagues into the interior. They have more than once devastated the Portuguese settlements on this part of the coast; and their different tribes are frequently at war amongst themselves; yet they are industrious and docile in a state of servitude; and individuals not unfrequently settle at Zanzibar or in the Comora isles. Their dress consists of a piece of blue cotton cloth wrapt round the loins; and both sexes decorate their arms and legs with bracelets of copper, iron, Venetian beads, or ivory. They also wear copper ear-rings. They practise circumcision; and recognise a good and an evil spirit,-the former by the name of Muluku, and the latter by that of Minepa or Mitoha. They work the iron-ore which their territory affords with considerable skill, and forge their own weapons and implements.-Froberville. AMA
AMAL, a town of Sweden, on the W side of Lake Wener, in N lat. 59°. Pop. in 1810, 843; in 1833, 1,457. It possesses some shipping, and conducts a small trade in timber, deals, and tar.
AMALAGAN, one of the Ladrones or Marianne group, in N lat. 18° 4', E long. 165° 24', 3½ m. from Guam.

AMALFI, a city and port in the kingdom of Naples, in Principato Citra, on the declivity of a mountain, 12 m. WSW of Salerno, in 40° 37' N lat., 14° 35' E long. It consists chiefly of one square, and a street which runs inland from it, gradually ascending and becoming narrower as the rocks close on each side, till it is not more than sufficient to allow of two mules abreast. It is the seat of an archbishop, and has a large gloomy cathedral. The inhabitants are good mariners; but its principal manufacture is the preparation of macaroni, which is said to employ onehalf of the pop. Remains of ancient walls and fortifications are visible on the impending heights of Tramontani. The pop. is variously estimated at from 3,000 to 11,000. In the Middle ages, A. had a pop. of 50,000, and was distinguished for its commercial navy, by which its inhabitants carried on an extensive commerce with Egypt and all parts of the then known world; and, though sadly fallen from its original importance, it still has some commerce. The testimony of ancient historians, and numerous vestiges of antiquity, lead to the belief that A. was of Roman origin. We are told that early in the 4th cent., when many noble families of Rome emigrated to Constantinople, a party from Ravenna was wrecked, first on the coast of Dalmatia, and subsequently near to Cape Palinurus, in the environs of Pæstrum, where they settled, and built a town at the mouth of the little river Molpha or Melfi: but finding this an insecure abode, it was soon abandoned, and the colony established itself upon a rock between Cape Tombeau and the promontory of Conque. Here they built a town surrounded by walls, and named Scala. Towards the middle of the 4th cent. some of these families took up their abode at the foot of the rock, and named their new establishment Amalfi, in memory of the town of their ancestors. A. was long under the protection of the Greek emperors, and ranked amongst the towns of greatest importance to the eastern empire, after Capua, Naples, Benevento, and Gæta. With the exception of the chief magistrate, the citizens of A. annually elected their own magistrates; but it was not until the close of the 9th cent. that this right was formally conceded to them. Insufficiently protected by the imperial governor, who constantly resided at Naples, A. was subjected to frequent inroads from the Lombards. The establishment of the consular government at A. contributed greatly to its prosperity. For their services in the crusades against the Saracens, the citizens of A. obtained the island of Capri, which remained for three centuries under their dominion. In 897 they elected a chief magistrate for life, with the title of doge. This officer was chosen from the nobility, and invested with unlimited power, except in maritime affairs. Mansone Fusili, the son of one of their last consuls, was the first doge of A. The present town of A. does not occupy above the sixth part of this once celebrated territory. The sea seems to have here worked with irresistible violence against the rocks, and desolated the coast. In 1013, a frightful tempest nearly destroyed the port and lower part of the town of A. This accident materially changed the aspect of the town whose port formerly occupied the space between Amalfi and Majuri. "Tradition speaks of a road from Majore to Concha, now under water, at a distance of at least 4 or 5 m.; and divers describe fountains and marble staircases which they have discovered several fathoms deep." [Athenaum.] The merchants of Amalfi and those of Venice are generally described as the earliest promoters of trade in Europe. Their relations with the East were early formed; and in the 11th century, Alexandria, Antioch, Joppa, Tunis, Tripoli, and Bagdad, were all visited by these enterprising men, while the industry and wealth of the Amalfitans were eulogized in poetry by William de Pouille. The sultan of Egypt sanctioned the establishment of the convent of St. Mary Magdalene at Jerusalem, and an hospital, which gradually came to be used as a magazine of merchandise. At a later period, Bohemond III., prince of Antioch, granted sites for three bazaars, and remitted his right of customs in favour of the Amalfitans. Similar advantages were afforded to the merchants of A., in Palermo, Messina, and all the cities of Italy. There are two popular traditions extant which go far to establish the celebrity of the Amalfitans as navigators and merchants. One of these is the discovery of the mariner's compass by Flavio Gioia-a citizen of this place-about the commencement of the 14th century; the other, the institution of a nautical code, styled 'Amalfitan Tables.' These traditions have long been the subject of scientific research and discussion. The existence of Gioia has been questioned; and Germans, French, and English, have each in their turn claimed the honour of the invention attributed to him. The probability is that Gioia derived the first idea of this instrument from some Eastern trader, it having been used by the Arabs and Chinese long before it was known in Europe. The tradition respecting the Amalfitan tables is equally obscure. In the 11th cent. A. had to defend itself against the Normans, the powerful auxiliaries of the dukes of Salerno and Benevento. But its most formidable foes were the Pisans. While the Amalfitan fleet was at Naples, in the service of Roger of Sicily, the consuls of Pisa, Alzopardo and Cane, forced the port of Amalfi and pillaged the town for three days. They returned in 1137, and, on this occasion, the town was completely reduced. It was at this time that a solitary copy of the Pandects of Justinian—a work long lost to the world—was discovered by the conquerors, amidst other plunder, within the walls of A.; and from that epoch the study of the Roman law revived in Europe. Finally, in 1343, nearly all that had been left undemolished in A. by the enemy was destroyed by a tempest. From this time, A. appertained by turns to the houses of Colonna and Orsino, and to that of the Piccolomini allied to Aragon. The Costiera D'Amalfi is that part of the gulf of Salerno extending from Cetara to Positano, which formed the territory of the ancient republic. In tracing this coast from Cetara, we have the little port of Erchia, at the foot of an immense rock, on which are the ruins of a temple of Hercules, on a deserted and seaworn coast. Capes Orso and Tumolo are projected from the semicircular chain of rocks which skirt the shore at this place. From the tower Del Cane, situated on the extremity of Capo d'Orso, we see the gulf of Amalfi, with the villages of Majuri, Minuri, and Atrani, overhung by lofty mountains, on which are numerous Gothic chateaus. Majuri is at the foot of the gulf, at the mouth of a river, but has no port. In the midst of the mountains which rise above this village is the fertile valley of Tramonti, comprising the district amongst the hills of Fallisco, Albino, St. Angelo, Ceriti, and Sparviero. The climate is delicious; and among the numerous hamlets scattered over this district are Polvica, Saint-Elia, Paterno, Figlino, Corsano, Cesarano le Pietre, Capitagnano, Campinola, Ponte, Gete, Novilla, and Bocara. This valley was defended on the N by the tower of Chiunso, built in 1457 by Raymond Orsino, prince of Salerno and grand feudatory of the duchy of Amalfi; and on the S by the castle of Majuri. In 1260 it was bestowed on the celebrated John of Procida. Minuri, about 2 m. from Majuri, was—in the Middle ages—the chief arsenal of Amalfi. This little town was first named Forulla and subsequently Rhegina Minor, and Majuri was Rhegina major. At Cape Tombeau the aspect of the country completely changes. We now see beautiful villages rising one above the other on the mountains, as Villamena sur Minuri, Ravello sur Villamena, and others. Beyond these, and at the entrance of a dark narrow valley is Attani, the birth-place of Masaniello, separated from Amalfi by a small promontory called Cape Amalfi. "Whoever desires to see one of the most beautiful and extensive views in all Italy, must climb up the very face of the mountains which form the background of A. -a good two hours' labour." From this point the coast is beheld circling round from the Campanella to Capo Licosa; the mountains of Apulia are seen in the distant horizon; and the ruined temples of Pæstum, if the atmosphere be clear; while beneath is Capo d'Orso, and behind it Salerno, and, clinging to the mountain, at your feet, is Amalfi.—Gibbon.—Swinburne's Travels.—Sismondi, Histoires des Rep. Italiennes. Tome 1er.—Istoria dell ant. rep. d'Amaifi. Napoli 1724, 2 vols. 4to.—Athenœum Journal, 1847.— Craven's Tour.
AMALIENHOF, a village of Prussia, in Brandenburg, 3 m. NE of Frienwalde. Pop. 258.

AMAMBAY (SIERRA DE), a range of mountains running from N to S through the southern part of the Brazilian prov. of Matto Grosso and the northern part of Paraguay, and constituting the water-shed between the Parana and the Paraguay. According to Mawe, they terminate to the S of the Iguatimy, where they form a ridge running E and W called Maracayer.

AMANA, a river of Venezuela, in the prov. of Cumana, flowing by a SE course into the W side of the gulf of Paria.—Also a lake of Portuguese Guayana, formed by the confluence of several streams branching from the Maranon on its N side, in the territory of the Carahyahys, and afterwards re-entering it; in about S. lat. 2° 30', W long. 64° 20'.
AMANCAES, a mountain in the vicinity of Lima, rising to an alt. of 2,880 ft. above sea-level.

AMANCE, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Aube, arond of Bar-sur-Aube. Pop. 529 .- Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Meurthe, arrond. of Nancy. Pop. 552.—Also a cant., com., and v., in the dep. of Haute-Saone. Pop. of the cant., which comprises 13 com. in 1831, 8,575; of the com. and v., which are 12 m. NW of Vesoul, 1,026.
AMANCEY, a canton, commune, and v. of France, AMA in the dep. of Doubs, arrond. of Besançon. The cant., comprising 23 com., had a pop. of 6,874 in 1831. The pop. of the com. and v. in 1841 was 626.

AMAND (SAINT), a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Antwerp, arrond. of Malines, on the r. bank of the Scheld, 12 m. W of Malines. Pop. 2,915. It has some trade in linen and cotton goods which are woven in the town and neighbourhood.—Also a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher, arrond. of Vendôme. The cant., comprising 14 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 5,985. The pop. of the com. in 1841 was 501.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Manche, arrond. of Saint-Lo. Pop. 1,357.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Marne, arrond. of Vitry-le-Francais. Pop. 1,209.

AMAND-LES-EAUX (SAINT), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Nord, arrond. of Valenciennes.—The cant., comprising 18 com., had a pop. of 31,288 in 1831.—The com. and v. are on the l. bank of the Scarpe, 7 m. E by N of Valenciennes. Pop. 8,734. It is an active trading and manufacturing district, especially in flax and linen.
AMAND-MAGNAZEIX (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, arrond. of Bellac. Pop. 1,288.
AMAND-DE-MONTPEZAT (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Lot-et- Garonne, arrond. of Agen. Pop. 1,404.

AMAND-MONTROND (SAINT), an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Cher.—The arrond, has an area of 264,967 hectares. Pop. in 1831, 91,732; in 1846, 103,723. It comprises 11 cantons: viz., Saint-Amand-Montrond, Charenton, Chateau-Meillaut, Chateauneuf-sur- Cher, Chatelet, Dun-le-Roi, La Guerche-sur-l'Aubois, Lignières, Nerondes, Sancoins, and Saulzais-le-Potier. The cant. comprises 12 com., and had a pop. in 1831 of 12,028.—The com. and town, which are near the Cher, at the embouchure of the Marmande, had a pop. of 7,747 in 1846. It is a neat town, and has a considerable trade in wine, leather, cattle, and wool.

AMAND-EN-PUISAYE (SAINT), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Nièvre.— The cant., comprising 6 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 7,393.—The com. and v., which are in the arrond. and 9 m. NE of Cosne, had a pop. of 1,690 in 1841.

AMAND-ROCHE-SAVINE (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Puyde-Dome.—The cant., comprising 5 communes, had a pop. in 1831 of 7,521.—The com. and town, which are in the arrond, and 6 m. W by N of Ambert, had a pop. of 2,204 in 1841.
AMAND-SUR-SE'VRE (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, arrond. of Bressuire. Pop. 1,470.

AMAND-TALLENDE (SAINT), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Puy-de- Dome.—The cant., which comprises 8 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 9,912.—The com. and v., which are in the arrond. and 8 m. S by E of Clermont, had in 1841 a pop. of 1,489.

AMANDA, a township in Hancock co., Ohio, U.S., on a branch of the Auglaize. Pop. 490.—Also a township in Fairfield co., in the same state, 38 m. SSE of Columbus, on Clear creek. Pop. 1,987.— Also a township in Allen co., in the same state, on the Auglaize. Pop. 280.
AMANDIN (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Cantal, arrond. of Murat. Pop. 1,308.
AMANDOLA, a town in the States-of-the-Church, on the Tenna river, 14 m. SE of Camerino.
AMANLIS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, arrond. and 8 m. S by E of Rennes, on the Seiche. Pop. 2,801.

AMANS (SAINT), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Aveyron .- The cant., which comprises 6 com., had a pop. of 6,006 in 1831.-The com., which is 12 m. N by W of Espalion, had a pop. of 1,098 in 1841.—Also a cant., com., and v. in the dep. of Lozère.-The pop. of the cant., comprising 10 com. in 1831, was 5,785.—The com. and v., 10 m. NW of Mende, had a pop. of 323 in 1841.

AMANS-LA-BASTIDE, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Tarn, arrond. of Castres.—The cant., comprising 6 com., had a pop. of 9,309 in 1831.—The com. and v., which are on the Thoré, had a pop. of 2,331 in 1841.
AMANS-DE-VALTORET (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Tarn, arrond. and 16 m. SE of Castres. Pop. 1,869.

AMANT-DE-BOIXE (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, arrond. of Angouleme. - The cant, comprising 17 com., had a pop. of 12,078 in 1831.—The com., 10 m. NW of Angouleme, had a pop. of 1,545 in 1841.
AMANT-DE-MONTMOREAU (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Charente, arrond. of Barlezieux. Pop. 1,151.
AMANTEA, a town of Naples, in Calabria Citra, 18 m. S by W of Cosenza, on the coast. Pop. 2,814. It is the see of a bishop, a suffragan of Reggio. Some identify this place with the ancient Nepezia.
AMANTO, a river of Peru, in the prov. of Cuzco, rising on the N side of the Cordillera Vilconata, and flowing into the Inambari, a little above Apuroma.
AMANUS. See Almadagh.
AMAPALLA. See Fonseca.

AMAPONDA, a Kaffir tribe of Southern Africa, whose territories extend from the Bashee river to the Umsikalia, 30 m. beyond the St. John river. They are upon the whole more industrious than their neighbours the Amakosa, cultivating the soil, and growing large quantities of maize, yams, and tobacco.
AMAR (RAS AL), a cape on the coast of Tunis, 2 leagues W of Cape Bon. It is the ancient Mercurii Promontorium.

AMARANTE, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Minho, on the Tamega, 23 m. SE of Braga. Pop. 4,000. AMARAPURA, [i. e. 'City of Immortals,'] a fortified city of the Burman empire, in the district of Jo or Gopyee, and prov. of Ava; in N lat. 21° 55', E long. 96° 7', about three-quarters of a mile from the left bank of the Irawaddi river, and 6 m. to the NE of Ava. To the N of the city is a romantic lake, called Tounzemahn, 7 m. in length, and 13 m. in breadth, which communicates with the Irawaddi, and during the rainy season stretches its expanse to the southern confines of the fort. To the E the landscape is bounded by a lofty range of hills, distant from 4 to 5 m. The city, formerly the capital of the empire, is an exact square, about 2 m. in circuit, and is entered on each side by one large central and two smaller gates. The rampart, exclusive of the parapet, is about 20 ft. in height, and is composed of earth, with strong brick facings. At each of the angles is a massive quadrangular bastion; and between these, at regular intervals of 200 ft., surmounting the gateways, and defending the intervening curtains, are eleven smaller bastions, with numerous embrasures. The ditch is of considerable breadth, and is crossed by a causeway defended by intrenchments. The streets of A. are spacious, and intersect each other at right angles. In the centre of the city, filling an area half-a-mile in circumference, is the royal palace, consisting of an irregular assemblage of wooden edifices, surmounted with domes covered with gilded copper, and enclosed with a palisade of teak planks. The houses, with few exceptions, consist of wood, with tiled roofs, ridged with earthen vessels, containing a constant supply of water for use in the frequently recurring cases of fire; those of the CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AMA better classes have palisade enclosures. The religious buildings are richly gilt, and present a splendid appearance. In the NW angle of the fort is the pudigant or royal library, deposited in curiously ornamented chests, about 100 in number, in the centre of a large paved court. The city is divided into four sections, each of which is under the distinct jurisdiction of a mayun or viceroy. The main channel of the river opposite A. is about 2 m. in breadth. In the dry season, this channel is filled with high sandy islands, which are all put under cultivation. The low situation of A., and the frequent inundations of the surrounding district, render the climate insalubrious to foreigners; but its environs are extremely fertile, producing wheat equal in quality to the finest in England.—This city was founded in 1783, by the Burmese monarch Mindaraju Pran; and previous to the retransference of the seat of government to Ava in 1819, consisted of from 20,000 to 25,000 houses, with a population estimated by Cox at 175,000. In 1827, the number of its inhabitants did not exceed 30,000. A considerable trade is said to subsist between A. and the inhabitants of Gunan in China.— Crawford's Ava.—Syme's Embassy.—Judson's Mission.
AMARGOS, an island of Chili, at the mouth of the Valdivia, in S lat. 39° 54'.
AMARGURA, an island in the S Pacific, one of the Friendly islands group, in S lat. 17° 57', W long. 175° 10′.
AMARI, a town of Greece, in Thessaly, on the r. bank of the Saranta, 6 m. S of Alassona.
AMARIBO, a river of French Guayana, flowing into the Atlantic, 9 m. NW of Maroni, after a course of 130 m.

AMARIN (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin.—The cant., comprising 16 com., had a pop. of 15,357 in 1831.—The com., 16 m. NE of Belfort, on the Thuren, had a pop. of 1,995 in 1841.
AMARO (Santo), a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 30 m. NW of Bahia, on the Sergipe-do-Conde. Tobacco, sugar, and manioc, are extensively grown in the environs, and exported from this place.

AMA'SIA, or AMASIYAH, a town of Asiatic Turkey, the cap. of the sanjak of the same name, in the pashalik of Sivas, in the prov. of Natolia, situated on the Tokat Su or Kizilirmak river, in N lat. 40° 35', E long. 36° 18′, 18 hours NW of Tokat, 45 m. SSW of Samsun, and 200 m. E of Constantinople. It is built between almost perpendicular rocks, or in the words of Strabo, in "a great and deep ravine," on the lower acclivities of the mountains of Djanik, and is intersected by the windings of the river, over which there is a fine stone and several wooden bridges. The town Fraser describes as "a strange, wild, rocky, peculiar place." The mountainous environs of A. render it inaccessible, except by two narrow passes on the N and S, which are so rugged as to be easily defensible. A strong castle, surmounting a lofty pyramid of rock, commands the town, and is the most remarkable object within its precincts. It is approached by a road cut out of the rock, and contains several vast circular chambers, which are supposed to have been the places of sepulture of the ancient kings of Pontus. The rays of the sun, concentrated by the surrounding precipices, create excessive heat during the summer-months, which often reaches 92° in the shade. A. is of considerable extent; but its streets are narrow and dirty; the houses are constructed of wood and of stone, -more generally of the former,—and are covered with tiles. The principal buildings are the mosques, of which one, distinguished by the name of its founder Bajazet, is a magnificent edifice, surmounted by two lofty minarets, and constructed entirely of hewn stone. The celestial college, built by the same prince, once a magnificent | structure, is now falling into ruins. The public baths are numerous and handsome, with fine promenades in front, adorned with trees and enclosed by high walls. They are supplied with water raised from the river by means of buckets, attached to the circumference of large wheels, some of them 30 and even 40 ft. in diameter, which are put in motion by the current. The environs of A. are filled with fruitgardens and mulberry plantations, and produce an excellent species of wine, resembling sherry, and fruits of various kinds in great abundance. The commerce of this town consists chiefly in raw silk, remarkable for its whiteness, and which supplies the manufactures of Damascus and Aleppo. It is worth about 370 or 380 piastres per 1,000 drachms [Bowring], hare-skins, wool, gall-nuts, tobacco, leather, madder, which is of good quality, jeree, or the yellow berry, used by dyers under the name of grains d'Avignon, and corn. Rock salt is dug in the vicinity. The only manufacture which it possesses is that of calico-printing. The pop. has been variously estimated at from 25,000 to 35,000. Vice-consul Suter estimated the number of houses in 1838 at 3,970, of which 3,500 were Turkish, 350 Armenian, and 120 Greek. Balbi carries the pop. so high as 50,000. The inhabitants generally are said to be distinguished for the urbanity of their manners and their hospitality to strangers, and the women are reputed as the finest and most agreeable in Asia Minor.—Amasia was originally a free town of Cappadocia. In the Middle ages it was the residence of the first Ottoman sultans, one of whom made choice of it for his capital. In the year of the Hegira 877, when Tokat was sacked by the king of Persia, it was under the governorship of Mustapha, son of Mahommed II. It is now the seat of a caimacan, its government having been separated from that of Sivas; and returns the Porte a revenue of 25,000 purses, or upwards of £100,000. It is the seat of a Greek bishop; and is celebrated as the birth-place of the famous geographer Strabo.—Geog. Journ., vol. x.—Correspondent of the Times in 1846.—Bowring's Report on Syria, 1840.— Missionary Researches in Armenia.—Fraser's Kurdistan.

AMASTRA, or AMASERAH, a town of Asiatic Turkey, on a rocky headland on the coast of the Black sea, 48 m. NE by N of Cape Baba, in N lat. 41° 45'. Pop. 800. It overlooks the sea to the N, but its greatest extent fronts the interior or S. On the E is a wide and capacious bay.
AMATA, a village of Syria, in the pash. of Damascus, on the Wadi Rajib or Hieromax, 3 m. W of the Jordan.—Also a v. of Lombardy, in the Milanese, 9 m. N of Milan.
AMATAKA, an island in St. George's channel, off the coast of New Britain, about 8 m. in length, with a breadth of 5 m. It was discovered by Cartaret, who named it York Island.
AMATAPE, a town of Peru, on the r. bank of the Chira, 60 m. N by E of Sechura.

AMATEMBU, a Kaffir tribe, residing on the Bashee river in South Africa, and extending inland as far as the Karroo desert, and to the N and W of the Amakosa. They are commonly called Tambookies, and are of similar habits to the Amakosa.
AMATHANTE, a village of Cyprus, on a stream of the same name, 5 m. NE by E of Limasol, on the site of the ancient Amanthus, of which numerous remains exist here.

AMATIQUA, or AMATIQUES, a town of Guatimala, on the W coast of a bay of the same name, in the gulf of Vera Paz, at the mouth of the Guanocos, in N lat. 15° 23', W long. 89°. AMATITA'N-softened AMATITLAN-a lake of Guatimala, in the prov. of Escuintla, to the SE of Old Guatimala. It is about 9 m in length, by AMA 3 m. in breadth, and is nearly enclosed by lofty mountains. It discharges its waters by the Michatoyat. It furnishes an inexhaustible supply of crabs, cray-fish, pepescas, moharras, and pescaditos. Numerous warm springs occur on its borders. Baily says that this lake was represented to him as fathomless at 50 yards from the shore. The v. of Amatitan, sometimes distinguished as San Juan Amatitan, stands upon the E bank of the lake. The wells of this v. are all of brackish water, having a mixture of alum and salt; in the suburbs, they are all hot, but free from any considerable mixture of minerals. Dunlop says: "In one, which I got opened in the Rincon—the site of most of the larger cochineal plantations—the heat became intense after 10 yards had been excavated; at 20, the ground thrown out was so hot as almost to burn my hands. Two men who had engaged to open the well abandoned it: at last I found a third who, for a high reward, undertook to follow it till he found water; which he did at 32 yards' depth, but actually boiling. The ground where this was situated was rather high; but in the low grounds, near the lake and river, boiling water is met with everywhere at the depth of 2 or 3 yards, and in many places rises spontaneously to the surface. Early in the morning, before sunrise, if the hand be placed upon the ground, it feels quite hot, and the steam may be seen ascending through the pores of the earth."
AMATO, a town of Naples, in Upper Calabria, 7 m. SE of Nicastro, on a river of the same name, which, rising in Mount Calistro, flows into the gulf of Santa Eufemia.
AMATRICE, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo Ultra 2da, in the district of Citta-Ducale, 20 m. N of Aquila, near the sources of the Tronto. Pop. 5,000.

AMAXIKI, or Santa Maura, the cap. of the Ionian island of Santa Maura, on the NE coast, in N lat. 38° 49', E long. 20° 42'. It stands on a narrow tongue of land, and owing to the shallows in the vicinity is unhealthy. In 1825, it suffered terribly by an earthquake. Pop. 6,000. The town is unwalled, but it is protected by a strong castle with which it communicates by a road raised on upwards of 300 arches, the remains of an ancient aqueduct.
AMAXURA, a river of Florida, U.S., rising in the Seminole swamps, near Lake Apopka, and flowing first NW and then SW to the gulf of Mexico, into which it falls after a course of about 120 m.
AMAY, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, on the Meuse, 6 m. NE of Huy. Pop. 2,470.

AMAYA, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. of Valladolid, 12 m. from Aquilha del Campos.—Also a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Sonora, 65 m. E of Culiacan.—Also a town of Bogota, on the Saldanha river, 110 m. SSW of Santa Fe de Bogota.
AMAYCUREE MOUNTAINS, a range in British Guayana, intersected by the parallel of 2° 10' N, in which some of the head-streams of the Rupununy take their rise.
AMAYCHA, or AMACHA, a town of La Plata, near the source of the Guachopas, on the W side of the Cordillera de los Valles.
AMAYUCA, a river of Ecuador, flowing through the territory of the Payaguas, into the l. bank of the Maranon, 12 m. below the confluence of the Napo.
AMAZIGH. See Berbers.

AMAZON, or MARANON, an immense river traversing nearly the entire breadth of the southern continent of America from W to E. It is called the Pa'RANATINGA, and the GUIENA, by the natives; and MARANON OF MARANHAM, ORELLA'NA, and AMAZON, by Europeans. It was first explored by Francisco Orellána, in 1539. Embarking from Quito on the Rio Coca or Napo, he met with a large river, the course of which brought him to the coast of Guayana, after a navigation which he estimated at 1,800 leagues. Between the mouth of the Rio Negro and of the Xingu, Orellána encountered a tribe of female warriors, and from this circumstance,-described with all the exaggerations of that taste for the marvellous common to the 16th cent., -is derived the name of

AMAZON, by which this river is commonly known. More properly, however, this name belongs only to that part of its course which is below the estuary of the Rio Negro; above that point, to the frontier of the Brazilian territory, it is known as the Rio Soli- MOES or SOLIMAO; and in the upper part of its course, the main stream, as far as Tabatinga, opposite the mouth of the Yavari, is called the Maranon [Lieut. Smyth], from a Spanish adventurer of that name who, in the year 1513, discovered the first great branch of the river which takes its rise in the Andes. The eastern sierras, or mountains of secondary elevation, which, running parallel with the central or highest ridge, and projecting branches far into the interior, serve as an immense buttress to the Andes of South America, are covered with stately and eternal forests. In these vast and elevated woodlands lie the sources of innumerable and mighty torrents, which, rushing down their woody slopes, and through the dark chasms and glens which sink between them, form, by their successive confluence, the majestic Amazon. The powers of description are unable to convey an adequate idea of the grandeur of this magnificent river; for, as the poet has sung, Scarce the Muse Dare stretch her wing o'er this enormous mass Of rushing waters; to whose dread expanse, Continuous depth, and wondrous length of course, Our rivers are but rills. From the 3d degree of N lat. to the 19th of S lat.,or from the source of the Cacaguana to that of the Madeira,—a space of 22 degrees in direct meridional distance, but of more than 2,000 m. by the windings of the mountain-chain, -not a single stream descends the eastern side of the Andes which does not contribute to swell the ocean-flood of this river, which, for length of course and volume of water, has no equal on the surface of our globe; which drains a forestplain of dimensions equal to half the extent of the African Sahara; and gathers its tributary waters from an area scarcely one-sixth less than that of all Europe, and consisting nearly of one immense plain, sloping gradually and almost insensibly to the Atlantic. Fortunately the tract immediately bordering the river is generally so far elevated above the channel as to protect the country from being converted into a swamp. The main trunk of this enormous stream is composed of three principal streams,-namely, the Apurimac, the Beni, and the Tunguragua, -each of which has, at different times, and by different geographers, been invested with the honours of the parent-stream. This is not surprising, as its head-waters are numerous and widely dispersed also on the eastern face of the Andes, and the mountainous regions in which they lie have been very imperfectly explored. Even yet, notwithstanding the great additional information derived from the map of La Cruz, and the voyages of Girval and Sobreviela, the remotest source of this river is by no means determined with the degree of accuracy necessary to remove all doubt. The Tunguragua, or river of Lauricocha, is generally regarded as the parent and principal stream, on the authority of Fritz, or Frezier, a Jesuit missionary, who first published a chart of its course in 1707, in which this river is represented as the true Maranon. The fact however is, that the Apurimac, or Ucayali of the Jesuits, is a prodigious river, under a parallel where the Tunguragua, or river of Lauricocha, is unborn. The Tunguragua.] The New or Upper Maranon, or Tunguragua, proceeds from the Alpine lake of Lauricocha, in the plains of Bombon, on the table-land of Pasco, in the district of Huanaco, in Peru, in S lat. 10° 30', and W long. 76° 30'. Its fountain-lake is only 3 m. in length, and 1 m. in breadth; but within a little distance of the point where it emerges from the lake its stream is 75 ft. in breadth and 3 ft. deep. Its course is for a short space NE, and then NNW, through a longitudinal valley formed by the eastern and the medial chains of the Andes. La Condamine has alleged, that whenever mountain-ranges are divided into parallel chains, the central must be the most elevated; but it is on the E of the western and only chain in this latitude which merits the denomination of lofty that this branch of the A. takes its rise. Throughout its course, from S to N through this valley, the Tunguragua is navigable by canoes; its falls do not commence until it turns E in its passage through the medial chain of the Andes; but for this interruption, however, which occurs in its descent from the mountainous region of the central chain, between San Jago and Tomependa, this river would be navigable by large vessels from its mouth to a distance of 120 m. from its source. At the point at which the Tunguragua takes an easterly direction near Jaen de Brancamoros, in S lat. 5°, and W long. 78° 40′, 420 m. from its source, it receives on the left the Rio Chinchipe, descending from the NW, and on the right the Chachapoyas or Huahua from the SE. From Jaen, it bends its course NE as far as the Pongo de Manseriche, or narrow pass of Borja, receiving, from the Andes of Quito, before it leaves the mountains, the San Jago, or river of Loja, after a course of 60 leagues from the NW. A little below the mouth of the San Jago, the Tunguragua passes the famous strait of Borja, where its breadth is suddenly reduced from 1,800 to 150 ft., between two high walls of rock, and where it runs with a rapidity calculated by Condamine at 9 m. an hour. It is observed by Humboldt, as a remarkable circumstance, that the great falls of the Amazon occur so near its source that fivesixths of its entire length are perfectly uninterrupted. The greater number of the pongos or falls of the Upper Maranon, from the confluence of the Chinchipe to the village of San Borja, consist of a series of stony dikes occurring at distant intervals. The most remarkable of these pongos is that of Manseriche, above Borja. "Sometimes," says Humboldt, "the tumultuous current of the rivers is to be ascribed to the extreme contraction of their channels: such is the case at the pongo of Manseriche, which Condamine believes much more dangerous than it really is." In fact, it may with more propriety be called a strait or canal than a fall. The cataracts of rivers, by the filtration of subterranean caverns, generally occasion the loss of a portion of the volume of their waters: at the pongo of Manseriche, it has been observed that not only the waters of the river itself, but even the timber which is floated down by them, is thus engulphed. This pongo cannot be descended in boats, which would be speedily dashed in pieces on the rocky bed and sides; balsas, or light rafts made of canes slightly bound together, are therefore used here. In these rafts, the different beams of which they are composed are neither dovetailed nor nailed together, but fastened by twigs called lianas, which are extremely flexible and elastic, so that when the raft strikes with violence against the rocks—which must frequently happen from the irresistible impetuosity of the current—the lianas have the effect of a spring, and by their elasticity so deaden the shocks that they occur unheeded. The greatest danger to which the navigator at this part of the A. is exposed, is the being carried by eddies out of the current, which he may find it almost impossible to regain. This accident happened to Condamine in the course of his adventurous navigation; and he relates that not a year before, a missionary thus drawn into the vortex was kept in it for two days, and might have perished for want but for a providential swell of the river which brought him again into the current! Condamine calculated, that in the narrowest part he was carried on his raft at the rate of 2 toises per second, which is equal to rather more than 9 m. an hour. After he had emerged from the jaws of the mountains on this raft, he describes the aspect of the country in the following terms:-"I found myself in a new world, apart from all human intercourse, on a fresh water sea, surrounded by a maze of lakes, rivers, and canals, penetrating in every direction the gloom of an immense forest which but for them were altogether impenetrable. New plants, new animals, and new races of men were here exhibited to my view. Accustomed during seven years to mountains lost in clouds, I was wrapt in admiration at the wide circle embraced by the eye, restricted here by no other boundary than the horizon, save where the hills of Pongo,— soon about to disappear,-raised themselves to chequer the constant monotony of the scene. To the crowd of varying objects which diversify the cultured fields of Quito, and which still presented themselves to my imagination, succeeded the most marked uniformity, all that was to be seen being verdure and water; for the earth is so thickly covered with tufted plants and bushes, that it is trod but not perceived; and to find a barren space, though but a foot square, would require a world of toil. Below Borja, and 400 or 500 leagues beyond, on falling down the river, a stone,— nay a pebble,-is an object rare as a diamond. The savages of these countries indeed have no conception of stones; and when, on visiting Borja, they first perceive them, the wonder they occasion is admirable to behold; every gesture tokens surprise; they collect them together, and load themselves with the precious burden, till, beginning to notice their abundance, they finally disregard, and throw them away." Generally, however, the pongos of the Amazon are very destructible, as their dikes consist of a friable breccia. Beyond the pongo of Manseriche to its mouth, no other rapid occurs in the course of the Tunguragua. Emerging from the longitudinal valley between the mountain group of Parimé, and the great mountain masses of Brazil, it flows across a vast plain in a general direction from E to W. Below Borja, it receives the Morrone, after a course of 180 m. SE; and a little farther down, the Pastaca, composed of numerous streams rising to the W of the volcano of Cotopaxi, falls into the Tunguragua, by three mouths—the chief entrance being 1,200 ft. broad—after a course of 400 m. Forty m. below the Pastaca, the great river Guallaga, or Huallaga, enters the Tunguragua from the S, in S lat. 5°, and W long. 74° 30'. At the point of junction, the Guallaga is 1,152 ft. wide, and 180 ft. deep. This latter stream issues from the lake of Chinquiacoba, and is frequently denominated the river of Guanuco, which town it passes in S lat. 10° 3'; receiving in its progress N, amongst a multitude of minor streams, the Monzon, in S lat. 9° 22', from the W; and in S lat. 7° 10', the Moyobambo. The central chain of the Andes separates the basin of the Tunguragua from that of the Guallaga; and the third, or most easterly chain, rises on the right bank of the latter river, whose upper course is nearly parallel to that of the Tunguragua. It rolls through a mountainous country, and with a precipitous course forming many rapids, till, at Ponguillo, it escapes the mountains, and enters the plains with a channel 1,200 ft. wide. Here it runs with serene majesty, and, owing to its freedom from shallows and other impediments, is freely navigable; while its banks, covered with lofty palms and trees of every leaf, are enlivened by numerous birds of the richest plumage. It is navigable to where it is joined by the Chinchao; above which point the course of both is too precipitous to be ascended; though the Indians-who are extremely expert in managing their balsas or rafts-descend them from some distance above. The course northward, below the junction, is easy both ways, running between low secondary ridges, which lie on the flank of the great chain of the Andes, and parallel to it. The districts thus traversed are of extreme fertility; and the tributary streams which feed the main trunk of the river, all descend from the richest mining districts. From Guanuco-where the Guallaga becomes navigable-to its junction with the Tunguragua, the sailing distance is 732 m., which is usually accomplished in fifteen days. The chief impediments to its navigation consist in drift timber, trees growing in the stream, and numerous snags, as they are called in North America, besides a great number of malpasos or 'bad passes,' i. e. rapids, which require the canoes to be unloaded. [Smyth.] After its junction with the Guallaga, the Tunguragua receives the Tigris, or Tigre, after a SE course of more than 300 m. Twenty leagues below, and sixty from the mouth of the Guallaga, the Tunguragua enters into confluence with the Ucayali. The Ucayali or Apurimac, or its confluent the Beni, will be considered as the true head-stream of the Maranon by all who conceive that the branch of remotest source from the mouth of a river is entitled to be regarded as its true main stem. The source of the Beni is stated by some authorities to be 2° 30' farther S than that of the Apurimac; while, on the other hand, the greater direct course of the Beni is counterbalanced by the more winding course of the Apurimac, so that the actual length of its navigation is much greater than that of the Beni. The Apurimac.] The Apurimac, according to La Cruz, rises in the intendency of Cuzco, to the S of the mineral mountains of Cailloma, in S lat. 16° 10', and W long. 71° 30', at the foot of the western ridge of the Andes which divides the district of Cailloma from the river and valley of Quilca, within 50 m. of the Pacific in direct distance, and 12 m. to the NE of the city of Arequipa. A small lake called Vilque is generally represented as forming its head-source; but it is supposed by some to have its source still more remote,-perhaps in S lat. 17°; for after being joined by the Monigote or Pariguana, in Cailloma, not far from its presumed source, it is so deep as to be impassable but by a bridge. After pursuing a NE course for 180 m., and receiving, besides the Monigote, the rivers of Aconcagua, Cuero, Gallqui, Ocororo, Condoroma, Pichigua, and Chica, descending from the heights of Canes and Canches, it turns SW for 60 m., and then NW to its junction with the Vilcamayo, a direct distance of 200 m. In this part of its progress, it receives the Pachachacha on the S, after a course of 150 m. from the snowy heights of Huando, through the provinces of Aimaraez and Abancay. The Pachachacha is composed of three streams which unite in Aimaraez. A little below its confluence with the Pachachacha, the Apurimac receives from the SW the Charcas, or Calcamayo, in S lat. 13° 10', after a course of 160 m. direct distance from the Andes of Contisuyu. Eighty miles below this confluence, the Apurimac receives from the SE, in S lat. 12° 15', the Vilcamayo, denominated also the Quillabamba, a river scarcely inferior to itself. The Vilcamayo, according to Estalla, rises in S lat. 16°, in the small lake of Villafro, in the narrow angle formed by the chain of Vilcanota and the metallic mountains of Cailloma, in the district of Collabuas. After running nearly 200 m. in a parallel direction with the Apurimac, from which it is separated by the ridge of Caillorna, passing by the city of Tinta, and receiving the Cambapata from the ridge of Lampa on the SE, it turns to the NW, and receiving from the SW the river of Cuzco, joins the Apurimac after a comparative course of 420 m. A little below the confluence of the Apurimac and Vilcamayo, the former receives, from the W, the Mantaro, or river of Jauja, from the mountains of Huancavelica, in S lat. 12° 6'. The Mantaro is a large and powerful stream rising in the lake of Chinchaycocha, in S lat. 11° 30', immediately to the S of the Guallaga, and has a winding course of at least 400 m. The Apurimac now turns to the NE for 180 m., when it is joined by the mighty stream of the Beni. In this part of its course it receives, in S lat. 11° 21', the Perene, or river of Tarma, which rising in the heights of Tarma, falls into the Apurimac, after an eastern course of 200 m. The Tambo rises in the chain of Vilcanota, and, running to the E of the Andes of Cuzco, falls into the Apurimac, in S lat. 10° 45', after a meridional course of 240 m. The Beni.] Three leagues, or 9 m. below the confluence of the Apurimac and Tambo, the former is joined by the Beni, or Paro, or Parobeni, in S lat. 10° 40'. From the source of the Apurimac, to this confluence with its rival and perhaps superior stream, the distance is 620 British m. The Beni has been described as rising in the heights of Sicasica, near the town of that name, in the province of La Plata, in S lat. 18° 40', and W long. 69°, 100 m. to the SE of the lake of Titicaca, and within 120 m. of the coast. But Pentland ascertained that the snow-topped range to the E of the lake of Titicaca, occupies the site thus assigned to the sources of the Beni; and it is probable that the sources of the Beni do not lie further S than the 14th parallel. Its course is almost due N; and it is augmented in its progress by a host of streams on either side, till it enters the Apurimac with a stream more than 2 m broad and several fathoms deep, and with such power and force as to propel the course of the Apurimac for a considerable space towards the Andes. Yet such is the amazing grandeur of these streams, that this river of 2 m. in breadth is only a branch of the Beni, which, in S lat. 13°, sends-off a large arm towards the E into the lake of Rogagado; whence issuing in three powerful streams-the Yutay, the Tefe, and the Coari-it pays a second prodigious tribute to the Maranon; while by a fourth branch, called the river of Exaltation, it yields a large accession to the Madeira. The lake of Rogagado is 30 m. long, by 15 m. broad; and it might, with much show of reason, be argued that the Beni ought in fact to be regarded as the principal stream of the Maranon, both as having a longer direct course, and as being at least equal in magnitude if not superior to the Apurimac, at their confluence, even after throwing-off the above-mentioned powerful streams to the Maranon and the Madeira. The direct course of the Beni is at least 660 m. to its confluence with the Apurimac. Previous to this junction, the Beni is joined, in S lat. 11° 30', by the Inambari, from the SE, after a course of 300 m. After the junction of the Apurimac and Beni, the united stream receives the appellation of the Apoparo, or Great Paro; and bending to the NW receives, in S lat. 8° 26', the Pachitea, composed of numerous streams issuing from the Montana Real. From the Perene to the Pachitea,—a direct distance of only 270 m.,—such is the number of streams descending from the Andes and their continuous ridges, no less than forty large rivers, including the Beni and Tambo, join the Apurimac. From the Pachitea, the Apoparo bends its course to the NE for 350 m. in direct distance, but 900 m. by the course of the river. In this space it contains 132 islands, and is joined by a vast number of tributary streams on both sides, among which are the Aguaytia, from the W, in 7° 35'; and from the E, the Manca in 7°; the Saraiacu in 6° 45'; and the Tapichi in S lat. 5°. The last runs parallel with the Maranon for 350 m. In S lat. 4° 25', and W long. 71°, the Tunguragua unites with the Ucayali, opposite the village of Joachim de Omaguas. At this celebrated confluence, the Ucayali is divided into three branches, the chief of which is not less than 55 toises, or 352 ft. deep. From the junction of the Tunguragua to that of the Beni, is 1,200 m. by water, according to Father Girval, though not above 600 in direct distance; and from thence to the source of the Beni, 600 m. in direct distance; and from the junction of the Beni to the source of the Apurimac, 620 m. The junction of the Ucayali with the New Maranon or Tunguragua-which is here at least half as broad again as the Ucayali, or nearly 2 m. across—terminates the upper basin of the Amazon, which comprises the greater part of Peru, the eastern portion of Upper Peru, and, on the other side, the southern district of the territory of the Equatorial republics. After this confluence, the Maranon for 20 leagues directs its course to the NE, and receives on the left, in S lat. 3° 24', the Rio Napo, which rises in the central chain of the Andes, not far from Quito, at the eastern foot of Cotopaxi, and, augmented by numerous streams, reaches the Amazon after a direct course of 500 m. At the point of confluence, the Napo is 600 toises, or 3,830 ft. broad; while the Maranon is 900 toises, or a mile and 480 ft. wide, and of prodigious depth. Below this junction, the breadth of the river increases so much that a single arm or branch is frequently 900 toises broad, and the waves in a storm resemble those of the ocean. From this to the sea—at least 1,700 m. direct distance—the Maranon receives on the N, the Iza Parana, after a course of 800 m. from the vicinity of Pastos, besides thirty other large rivers, amongst which, in S lat. 3°, is the Caqueta, or Yapura, a river of great magnitude, having a direct course of 900 m. Except the Rio Negro, this is the largest stream which joins the Maranon, and owing to its rapidity, it would be quite unnavigable, if the immense body of water which it rolls were not broken by numerous islands. Condamine is wrong in saying that the Yapura enters the Maranon by eight channels, which, in consequence of his authority, have been so placed in all the late maps except Arrowsmith's. Ribeiro, who officially visited the spot in 1773, ascertained that it has but one mouth. The three channels above it are streams flowing out of the Maranon into the Yapura, called the Auatiparana, the Manhama, and the Uasanapu. These channels, which sully by their mixture the pure waters of the Yapura, greatly facilitate the navigation; there is here neither danger nor difficulty, boats glide in perfect safety with the current, or are impelled against it by the slightest touch of the oar. The four channels below the Yapura flow from two lakes. The Yapura is connected with the Iza Parana, and with the Rio Negro, in the upper part of its course, by branches communicating with each of these rivers. The shores of the Maranon are generally low from the Ucayali to the Rio Negro; and, except where broken by the mouth of tributary streams, present one continued mass of forest-trees matted together with creepers. [Smyth.] The Rio Negro.] The next great affluent is the famous Rio Negro,-a stream of prodigious magnitude. It rises in about N lat. 2° 30', and W long. 74°, and runs first NE and then SE, in a course of 1,260 m. In the upper part of its course, it is called the Ueyeneya; its native appellation is Guainia or Curana. It is called the Rio Negro, or 'Black river,' by the Portuguese, from the darkness of its waters, which is imputed by Maw to their being impregnated with iron; and by Rodriguez to their actual pellucidness rendering the black rocks beneath visible. The Rio Negro receives a host of tributary streams. Communicating, on the one hand, with the Orinoco, by means of the Cassiquiari, which branches-off to the NNE, -and on the other, by means of an arm, with the Yapura, which again communicates in a similar manner with the Iza Parana, -a person may sail from the Maranon, to the respective mouths of these three rivers, up these to the Orinoco, and down that stream to its mouth, and thus encompass all Guayana without ever setting a foot on shore. The Rio Negro receives, not far from its mouth, the Parimé or Rio Branco, from the NE, after a SSW course of 600 m. direct distance. At their confluence, the conflict of the Rio Negro and Amazon is tremendous, the former rushing right across the channel of the latter; and for twenty leagues, the dark waters of the Rio Negro may be distinguished from those of the Maranon. A little above the point of confluence-for it enters the Maranon by two mouths, thus forming an island—the Rio Negro is 1,203 toises, or nearly a mile and a half broad, at low water; in different parts of its course it spreads to the width of seven or eight leagues. So early as 1539 the imposing dimensions of the Rio Negro, at its confluence with the Amazon, was dilated upon by Orellana; but another century elapsed before any attempt was made to trace its origin to the declivity of the Cordilleras. Its medium breadth near Maroa is 200 or 250 toises. It is characterised, in the upper part of its course, by absence of sinuosity. Hence it is, says Humboldt, "like a great canal, carried in a straight line through a dense forest. In every change of direction it presents to the eye a vista of equal length." Its banks are high, but level and rarely rocky; they are uninhabited, except in the hilly country of the Manivas and Poignaves Indians. The source of the Inirida, a tributary of the Guaviare, is alleged by the natives to be only 2 or 3 leagues distant from that of the Guainia, as the Rio Negro is called at Zarita and Maroa; and Humboldt conceived that a portage might be established between them. On the upper part of the Rio Negro are the forts of Manoa, Tomo, Davipe, San Carlos, San Felipe, San Joze de Marabitanas, the towns of San Joao Baptista do Mabe, de San Marcelino, near the mouth of the Guaisia or Uexie, of Nossa Senhora da Guia, San Miguel de Iparana, San Felipe near the Rio Icana of San Joaquim do Coane, at the confluence of the famous Rio Uapes, and of San Gabriel de Cachociras. Besides numerous villages, there are on the lower Rio Negro the six towns of Thomar, Moreira, near the Rio Demenene or Uaraca, Barcellos opposite the confluence of the Rio Biubiu, San Miguel del Rio Branco, Moura, and Barra do Rio Negro. The population of the banks of this tributary of the Amazon exceeded, at the period of its navigation by Humboldt, that of the Upper and Lower Orinoco, Cassiquiari and Atabapo; in consequence, he supposed, of the difference in the character of the political institutions and colonial regime of the Portuguese and Spaniards. The long contested question of the fact of the existence of a connection between the Amazon and Orinoco was satisfactorily determined by Humboldt. The Cassiquiari (Guxiquiare), a branch of the latter river which runs from N to S, was ascertained by him to fall into the Guainia or Rio Negro, above San Carlos, in N lat. 2° 2', and W long 70°. Thus by going up the Orinoco as far as the Esmeralda, and then descending the Cassiquiari, the Rio Negro and the Amazon; or -in order to avoid approaching the confluence of several rivers which fall into the Orinoco near St. Fernando de Atabapo-by quitting that river at the point at which it makes a sudden detour from a westerly to a southerly direction, and ascending a series of little black rivers, the Atabapo, Tenci, and Tininini,—and crossing an isthmus of 6,000 toises, or 12,789 yards in breadth, to Cano Pimichin, a small stream which falls into the Rio Negro, a communication between Angostura, the capital of the ancient Spanish Guayana, and Great Para, can be achieved in the short period of 28 days. By the former route it occupies from 50 to 60, which is likewise objectionable from the force of the current of the Cassiquiari, the difficulty of obtaining provisions, and the swarms of mosquitoes with which it is infested. The completion of the former method of communication, by means of a canal across the portage, between the Spanish Orinoco, and the Portuguese territory of the Amazon, was proposed by Humboldt to Charles IV. The southern chain of the Parime range, a district of small extent, but rich in vegetable productions, separates the waters which belong to the Orinoco, Cassiquiari, and Rio Negro. The rivers on the S, or the affluents of the Rio Negro, are the Cababuri and Padaviri. The first (the Cavaboris, Cababuris, Cabury, Canhabury, and Catabuhu of the maps) divides near its source into two branches, of which the most easterly, known by the name of Baria, runs in a westerly direction, and forms successive junctions with the Pacimoni, Cassiquiari, and Rio Negro. The Cababuri falls into the Rio Negro near the mission of Nossa Senhora das Caldas. Lower down, between Carvoeyro and the town of Moura, is the mouth of the great Rio Branco or Parime, formed by the Uraricoera and Tacut. This river, which is the Queecuene of the natives, forms at its confluence with the Rio Negro, between its principal trunk and a small branch named Amayanau further to the E, a very narrow delta. Inferior tributaries.] Below this, many considerable rivers from the mountains of Tumuracag, descend into the Amazon; but they are all of inferior importance to those already mentioned. Yet numerous and powerful as are the streams which, from the N, contribute to swell this majestic river, those from the S are perhaps still more so. Among these are the Yavari, which forms the boundary betwixt Brazil and Peru. Plaza, a missionary of Yarayacu, is of opinion, from the information of the natives, that the Yavari is identical with the lower part of the Beni; but subsequent explorations have disproved this idea. At a short distance from its mouth the Yavari divides into several branches, and ceases to be navigable. A little above this river, and on the left side of the Maranon, in S lat. 4° 19', W long. 70° 17' [Smyth], is Tabatinga, the first Brazilian post on that river; lower down, on the right, is the parish of St. Paul. Tabatinga is now a joint possession of the Portuguese and Spaniards. Near San Paul de Olivenca, which stands on a rising ground, at a short distance from the Amazon, the islands formerly inhabited by the Omaguas and Cambevas, begin to appear, and the bed of the river becomes considerably expanded. Presenting an extensive surface to the action of the wind, it is often so tempestuous here as to submerge canoes overtaken by its sudden violence. Maw describes himself as drifting down the river below Tabatinga, with a current of 3 to 4 m an hour, on a miserable raft, for three days and nights, with nothing to guide him in the "pitch dark" night, or to break the deep silence, save the noise of beetles, the hoarse croaking of frogs, and "the loud mournful kind of crow of the night-bird." In the most southerly latitudes of the valley of the Amazon a strong wind sets in daily, two hours after the sun attains the zenith, which blows constantly against the current, but only becomes perceptible in the bed of the river. Below San-Borja, it assumes the direction of an east wind, and at Tomependa it blows between N and NNE. In the province of Brancamoros, more particularly, this Atlantic breeze sometimes assumes tempestuous violence; and it is not improbable that it is to the constancy with which it blows, and to the concurrence of the direction from W to E of the long course of the Amazon, the remarkable salubrity of the woody banks of this river, compared with those of the Upper Orinoco, is owing. Beyond St. Paul, the banks of the A. become high and steep, and rise in craggy masses of red argillaceous rock. The action of the river occasions frequent variation in the number and dimensions of its islands in this part of its course. One of the most extraordinary phenomena observed in this part of the Maranon, and one well-calculated to convey an idea of the vast volume of its waters, is the existence of three currents in its broader passages,—one on either side, and the third in mid-channel,—the water between these currents does not appear to flow rapidly. Below Castro de Avalans, the Maranon receives on the l. the great river Putumago, which, like the Napo, descends from the environs of Quito; and on the r. the Jutahy or Jutai, and the Hyuruha or Jurua; again, on the l., before arriving at the confluence of the Rio Negro, the Yupura or Japuro; and on the r. the Teffé, the Coary, and the Purus. The tributaries on the r. water the Brazilian province of Solimoens, and give their names to the principal districts which they intersect. The Jutahy or Jutai is 300 toises in breadth at its mouth, but neither its source nor those of the Teffé and the Coary have yet been discovered, nor is the number of the principal affluents of these rivers yet known. Egas, a town containing 400 inhabitants, is situated on the Teffé, about 6 m. from its mouth; on the opposite side is Puebla de Nogueyra, a town of nearly equal size. These places carry on trade with Para by means of vessels of 40 tons burthen, which can only accomplish the voyage twice in a year. The waters of the Teffé are clear and deep, but dark coloured. Below Egas its channel contracts, and it falls into the Maranon by two embouchures, a little below Egas. The Maranon forms, on its right bank, a lake named Peixi-Cuna, 3 m. in length, by 11 m. in breadth, which is filled with alligators and various kinds of fish, and is connected with the river by an igarape or strait. The Purus is a river of considerable extent, scarcely inferior, according to the testimony of the Indians, to the Maranon itself, Its source has not yet been discovered, but it is supposed to be between the Cordillera de Vilcanota and the eastern portion of the mountains of Carabaya, whence descend so many of the rivers in which gold is found. The Purus was ascertained by Thadeus Haenke to be identical with the river described to him by the Indians under the name of Manoa, as flowing through a very woody country, at a distance of about 10 days' journey from the Beni, and in size greater than that river. The breadth of the Maranon below the embouchure of the Purus, at those points of its course which are not interrupted by islands, is estimated by La Condamine at 1,200 toises, or 2,557 yards; and at the same places he was unable to find bottom with a sounding-line of 103 toises, or 219 yards. The Madeira, or Wood river-so called from the vast quantity of timber which Texeira saw floating down its stream—enters the Amazon 110 m. below the Rio Negro. This is the most important secondary river in South America, in respect of the length of its navigable course, and its facilities for communication with the interior. The Madeira rises on the E side of the eastern chain of the Andes, to the SE of the source of the Beni, and runs through the valley of Gupai el Grande, which is separated from the valley of Chuquisaca, and course of the Cachimayo, a tributary of the Pelcomayo, by the cordillera of Chuquisaca on the S, and by the snowy cordillera of Cochabamba on the N, which separates it from the beds of the Beni and the Mamoré. It is here called the Rio Grande, and the Guapaix, and runs to the E; it then takes a sweep round to the N and NW, piercing the eastern and lower prolongation of the cordillera of Cochabamba, and receives in its course the Apere, or San Miguel, and the Mamoré from the southern side of the same range, which latter stream communicates its name to the Rio Grande or Guapay. The Sararé, and the Guaporé, rise in the captaincy of Matto Grosso, in the interior of Brazil, within 3 leagues of each other, in the Campos des Parecis, the highest ground in Brazil, and the southern limit of Matto Grosso. This tract ends in a chain of mountains of the same name, extending 800 m. NNW, the same probably with the cordillera of Cochabamba. Thirsty, however, as the soil is, it is everywhere intersected with streams; and here the Tapajos, the Paraguay, and the Madeira, have some of their remotest sources. The Sararé is navigable from the place where it leaves its native mountains till it joins the Guaporé. It is full of islands, with flooded savannahs on both sides; and is covered with a species of floating weed which must be cut away with hatchets before any boat larger than a canoe can make its way. Its navigation is also much impeded by trees which have been undermined by the stream, or loosened by the inundations. Its breadth at its junction with the Guaporé is 200 bracas, or Portuguese fathoms of 7 ft. 2.30 in. each = 1,443 ft. Twelve miles below the junction of the Sararé and Guaporé is the town of Villa Bella, the capital of Matto Grosso in 1753. The united stream is now called the Itenas. Twenty miles below the ancient mission of San Miguel, the Rio des Baures enters the Itenas; and three days' sail below this confluence, on the left side, is that of the Ubay and Guaporé. Ten days' voyage up the Ubai Magdalena, or Itonamas—as it is called in Arrowsmith's map—is the ancient village of Santa Maria Magdalena, which gave its name to the river. These great streams traverse a country rich in every gift of nature; and, according to Haenke, the productions of their banks have almost an exclusive character of excellence. The second largest specimen of pure gold yet found, came from the country traversed by the main-branches of the Madeira; and large specimens, all of peculiar purity, are not unfrequently found in this district. The cocoa grown here is infinitely superior to that of Guayaquil. The finest quality of Peruvian bark is only found on the eastern slope of the Andes; indigo, cotton and rice, sarsaparilla, gums of all sorts, balsams, and the most fragrant spices of vanilla, are in extraordinary abundance; and the endless forests which line the shores of all these rivers abound in the finest timber for all uses, especially ship-building, while, at the same time, the trees distil aromatic and medicinal gums. At the junction of the Mamoré, and Guaporé or Itenas, the Mamoré is 500 bracas, or 3,609 ft. broad, with a depth of 50 ft.; the Itenas is still wider, but of less depth, with a clear stream. The Mamoré, on the contrary, is a turbid stream, and so rapid that it rushes across its rival stream, and dashes violently on the opposite bank. The confluent stream is called the Madeira. Above the junction, the Mamoré is so rapid that crocodiles are unable to make way up the stream unless they swim very deep. About 133 Portuguese leagues, or 532 m. below the fort of the Prince of Beira, are the great falls of the Madeira, nineteen in number. Of these the eighteenth cataract is the most formidable; the river being full half-a-mile wide, and augmented by the large stream from the lake of Rogagado, called the river of Exaltation, having a fall of 100 ft. This cataract, though not equal in height to Niagara, discharges more water than either it or that of the Missouri,— perhaps more than any known cataract in the world. At this fall is a steep portage of three quarters of a mile. The first of the falls, in ascending, causes a portage of one-third of a mile; some other falls cause still greater difficulties; and a portage at the fifth fall occasions a labour of four days. From above the confluence of the Madeira and Beni, to the foot of the great cordillera, the river presents a succession of falls and rapids. At the junction of the Beni and Madeira, the former is 800 bracas, or 5,733 ft. broad. From such a vast accession of tributary water, we may judge what an immense body must be carried down the great cataract. Ninety-six miles below the town of Borba, the Madeira enters the Amazon by several mouths, thus forming a delta. The main or straight branch is 5,733 ft. broad; the adjoining country is low, swampy, and uninhabitable during the inundations of the Madeira. The whole distance down the Madeira to the Amazon, from the fort of the Prince of Beira, is 1,280 m. Lower Tributaries.] The Topayos, or Topaysu, a stream of the first rank, descending from the mineral mountains of Matto Grosso, in Brazil, and parted by a ridge from the source of the Paraguay, in S lat. 13° 30', falls into the Amazon, 120 m. below the Bosphorus, or strait of the river, after a comparative course of 800 m.—The Shingu or Xingu, enters the Amazon 300 m. below the mouth of the Topayos, by the course of the river, after an absolute course of 1,200 m. from the Brazilian mountains. This stream is navigable two months' voyage up. At the junction, the Shingu is 6 m. broad; and the breadth of the Amazon is so great that the opposite bank cannot be seen. Below this, the Amazon towards its embouchure divides into two great branches, of which the northern is the broadest and retains its name; the other communicates with the Rio des Bocas by the strait of the Tanajopuru.—The Great Brazilian river, Tocantin, falls into the Grande Para branch of the Amazon, with an entrance still wider than that of the Rio des Bocas. This large stream is composed of the Araguaya and Tocantin, both of which, rising in 19° S lat. and joining in 6° S lat., run in one ample stream for 500 m., till they enter the Grande Para, in 1° 40' S lat., 20 leagues W of the city of Para. The whole course of the Tocantin is 1,200 m., exclusive of its windings; and it is navigable to a distance which requires three months to ascend. Mouth of the Amazon.] Below Para, which is 80 m. from the ocean, the Amazon, with a strong current, and by broad and deep channels branching through a labyrinth of islands, enters the sea between Cape North and the Punta el Baxos: the total breadth betwixt these two points being 180 m., and the average depth 175 fath., or 1,050 ft. With such violence does this enormous assemblage of waters enter the ocean, that it repels its waves, and carries its own stream pure and unmixed to the distance of more than 40 leagues. This circumstance is the more remarkable, as it is calculated that from Obidos, 400 m. in direct distance from the mouth of the Amazon, the ground does not decline above 4 ft.; while there is only 1,026 ft. of descent from Jaen de Brancamoros, a direct distance of 1,860 geographical, or 2,145 British miles from the mouth; and at the falls of Rentema, at some distance above Jaen, the elevation is only 1,240 ft. above the level of the sea. Condamine has calculated the descent of the river at 63 inches per mile, in a straight line, which, allowing for its windings, would be reduced to about 4 inches per mile. This is the same amount of fall as that of the Ganges between Hurdwar and the sea, a distance of 1,350 m. And, in the case of the latter stream, this descent gives a rate of motion something less than 3 m. an hour in the dry, and from 5 to 6 m. an hour in the wet season. [Rennell.] The tide rises and falls from 30 to 36 ft. at the mouth of the river, according to Texeira; but only 10 ft., according to others. Near the mouth, the bore rises from 12 to 15 ft. in height; and its noise is heard at the distance of 6 m. "This phenomenon is called, from its aboriginal name, pororoca, and gives character to the navigation of the A. for hundreds of miles. No sailing craft can descend the river while the tide is running up: hence, both in ascending and descending, distances are measured by tides. For instance, Pará is three tides from the ocean, and a vessel entering with the flood must lie at anchor during two ebb-tides before she can reach the city. Canoes and small vessels are sometimes endangered in the commotion caused by the pororoca; and hence they generally, in anticipation, lay-to in certain places called esperas or restingplaces, where the water is known to be but little agitated. Most of the vessels used in the commerce of the Upper A. are constructed with reference to this peculiarity of the navigation, being designed for floating on the current rather than for sailing before the wind, although their sails may often be made serviceable. The ebb and flow of the tides in the A. are observed with regularity six hundred miles above the mouth, at the confluence of the Madeira. The pororoca is much more violent on the northern side of the island of Marajó, where the mouth is wider and the current more shallow. It was well-described by Condamine a hundred years ago in these terms: 'During three days before the new and full moons, the period of the highest tides, the sea, instead of occupying six hours to reach its flood, swells to its highest limit in one or two minutes. It might be inferred that such a phenomenon could not take place in a very tranquil manner. The noise of this terrible flood is heard five or six miles, and increases as it approaches. Presently you see a liquid promontory twelve or fifteen feet high, followed by another, and another, and sometimes by a fourth. These watery mountains spread across the whole channel, and advance with a prodigious rapidity, rending and crushing everything in their way. Immense trees are instantly uprooted by it, and sometimes whole tracts of land are swept away." [Kidder. | This phenomenon is chiefly observable at Cape North, on the mouth of the Aurauari. Islands, &c.] Throughout its whole course, this mighty stream is studded with islands; many of these are from 4 to 5 leagues in circumference; not a few of 10 or 20; and the great island of Tupimambas exceeds 100 leagues in circuit. Most, if not the whole of these, are overflowed with the freshes and swells which come on with the periodical rains, and which are very great. During the inundations, an extensive tract of country is overflowed; many of the numerous islands change their situations; and others are formed by the new channels which the river in its boundless impetuosity is wont to make for itself. In the southern part of the estuary of the A. are the Isles de Briques, consisting of nuclei of granite rocks, of no great elevation, but rising above the level of the highest tides, and covered by alluvial deposits. The same peculiarity of structure is presented by the island of Mischiane, or Maxiana, on some parts of that of Marajo, and apparently, from its general similarity to these islands, by Cavienna. It may thus be inferred that near the embouchure of the A. the Atlantic ocean formerly presented a little granitic archipelago, running in a northerly direction along the coast. Some of these islands are now conjoined with the continent; others, constantly enlarging by accessions of alluvial deposits, are only separated from it by canals gradually diminishing in dimensions. Thus, says M. Reynaud, notwithstanding the changes produced by the operation of the tides, the delta of the Amazon presents a phenomenon similar to that of the Nile in the conjunction of the isle of Pharos with the adjacent continent. An extensive and remarkably uniform alluvial tract, chiefly composed of fine clay debritus carried down by the numerous streams belonging to that region of America and especially by the A., stretches from the mouth of the river to the bay of Ayapok, in the precise line of direction in which the ocean-currents carry its waters.
