The elevation of these alluvial tracts is seldom greatly above sea-level, but their central parts in their greatest extent always rise above the highest tides. They seem to have been upheaved either by the action of earthquakes, to which we know many parts of the S American coast have been subject, or by the more gradual process of alluvial deposits; or more probably, by the combined operation of both of these causes. In the islands in the embouchure of the A., and especially in Mischiane and Marajo, alluvial tracts frequently rise to the height of 18 or 20 ft. above the level of the river. In the eastern part of the Isles de Briques, and in a series of islands not marked in the maps, which run along the coast of the N Cape, they are so low and flat as to be all, excepting their taller vegetation, under water at every full tide. The size of these islands appears from notices by M. Reynaud greatly to exceed that given in the ancient maps. Thus the Isles de Briques, separated from one another by canals from 300 to 600 m. in breadth, form a group stretching from E to W over an extent greater perhaps by 8 or 9 m. than that represented in the maps. Such changes, whether in the production of new or in the enlargement of old islands, common to all rivers whose waters are infiltrated with alluvium-such as the Ganges—are peculiarly likely to occur in the delta the enormous masses of its waters, and from the shallowness of the entire line of the American coast from Cape Orange to Cape North. The banks of the A. are covered with immense and impenetrable woods which afford a haunt to jaguars, bears, leopards, wildboars, and an infinite variety of snakes and serpents. The river is deep at the very edge of the stream, without those sloping descents which characterize other streams; and it swarms with alligators, even as far up as Jaen de Brancamoros, and the Guallaga. It is also stored with an infinity of fish and amphibious animals, amongst which the turtles are held in great esteem, being reckoned the most delicious that are known. From the point where the Maranon leaves the Andes to its mouth, the vast country washed by its waters is level and featureless, but rich in vegetable productions. For the space of two months that Condamine sailed down its stream, not the smallest eminence was to be seen, till he beheld, at a great distance, the mountains of Tumucurag, in French Guayana, running east and west. Length of course, &c.] As to the length of course of this noble river, its direction being for more than one-third of its progress from SE to NW, it considerably exceeds the whole breadth of South America, even where widest. Condamine computed its navigation, from Jaen to the sea, at 1,000 marine leagues, or 3,000 Geographical miles, equivalent to 3,460 British miles including the windings; or, in a direct line, 2,145 m. But if the length be taken from the mouth of the Grand Para, or river of Belem, to the junction of the Amazon and Tunguragua, the distance, not including the windings, is at least 1,840 m.; and from that junction to the source of the Beni, 1,260 m.; giving a total of 3,100 m.; and if to this be added the windings and sinuosities of its navigable course, its absolute length cannot be less than 5,000 m. If the countries watered by this mighty stream were peopled by industrious and commercial nations, ships of 500 tons might ascend it for upwards of 4,500 m.; a frigate of some force might awaken, with its artillery, the echoes of the stupendous Andes, and alarm those vast upland deserts which the early discoverers of America, viewing as far beyond the din and stir of mortals, styled, in the play of imagination, "the wilderness of supreme repose." The extent of inland navigation opened up into the interior of South America by the A. and its innumerable tributaries, far exceeds that of the Missouri with its accessory streams: more than one-half of this vast continent might enjoy the advantages of a maritime shore, from the numberless mighty streams which swell the vast volume of the Amazon. By the Rio Negro a communication is opened with the Orinoco and the Caribbean sea; by the Tunguragua, with Quito and Guayaquil; by the Apurimac, with Cuzco; by the Guallaga, with Lima; by the mighty Beni, with Potosi; by the Madeira, with the Beni; and with this latter, by the Jutai, Teffé, and Coari; by the Madeira, Shingu, and Tocantin, with the Paraguay, Parana, and the Atlantic; and by the Perima and other navigable streams, with Spanish, British, and Dutch Guayana. The sources of the Guallaga are only 4 or 5 leagues distant from those of the Rio Huaura, which runs into the Pacific. The river of Jauja, or the Mantaro, rises near the source of the Rio Rimac, or river of Lima. The height of the cordillera, and the nature of the ground, render the execution of a canal impossible; but a commodious road might be constructed from Lima across the intervening ridge to the Guallaga, which would facilitate the transport of goods to Europe. The great streams of the Guallaga and the Apurimac would carry, in little more than six weeks, the productions of Peru to the mouth of the Maranon; and, in three months, from Lima to of the A. from the action of the different currents of Madrid; while a passage of four months is requisite to convey the same goods to the same point, in doubling Cape Horn. The cultivation of the fine country situated to the E of the Andes, and the wealth and prosperity of its settlers, depends on the free navigation of the Amazon. This liberty was denied by the Portuguese, who would not allow a Spanish vessel to go down the river below the Teffé; so that all communication between the coast and the country on the eastern declivity of the Andes, was carried on by post. "Some years ago a company was formed at New York, for the express object of introducing steam-boats upon these magnificent waters. The first boat was fitted up and sent out at a considerable expense, but the enterprise failed for want of co-operation on the part of Brazil. Ever since the revolution of 1835 was suppressed, the jealousy of Rio Janeiro towards the northern provinces has been incessantly manifested, and has led to the most pernicious restrictions on trade and intercourse. It has closed several ports to foreign commerce altogether, and in various ways prevented the national development of its commercially-rival cities. This jealousy has also tended to arrest the progress of the inland navigation of the Amazon and its tributaries. A vast mine of wealth for future adventurers lies in these waters; but not until the government repudiates its present policy and adopts the broad principles of free commerce can it expect to see them covered with the ships of all nations, bound on their mission of peace and civilization. The agricultural produce of the whole of the fertile and thicklypopulated slopes and plains on the east of the Andes might be transported on their bosoms to the Atlantic; and the Amazon might become the great highway from Europe to the important republics of Peru and Ecuador, and even to Bolivia and New Granada. The regulations of the government lie at the root of all that impedes the growth and blasts the prosperity of Para; let the blunders be retrieved, and nature would soon assert her redeeming power. A more active life and a better form of civilization would soon find their way into so favoured a region,—and the nation on the banks of the Amazon might gradually become the centre of enlightenment for the whole southern continent of America." [Daily News, 1847.] The territory watered by the Amazon and its branches is at least equal in extent to Continental Europe, and double that watered by the Missouri and its tributaries, being 2,500,000 sq. m. upon the most moderate calculation; and comprehending from the 20° of S lat. to the 2° and 3° of N lat., and from the 47° of W long. at the mouth of the Grand Para, to Jaen, in 78° 30', or 31½ degrees of longitude. Its navigable course possesses numerous advantages: it is never impeded with ice, as is often the case with the Upper Missouri,—there are no moving sands or sand-bars in its whole course, -it is navigable throughout the whole year,—and it has no shelving shores to render it dangerous to vessels. Besides these advantages peculiar to the river itself, so strong an easterly wind always blows up the stream from the Atlantic, that vessels are even carried by it against the stream. Yet, notwithstanding of these advantages, this majestic river still rolls its mighty course through regions unknown to civilization and to fame; where science has never shed its cheering rays to dispel the gloom that broods on its shores: and where no native historian or bard has ever arisen to immortalize its stream. The Mincio and the Scamander, though insignificant streams, are immortalized by the strains of a Homer and a Virgil, and are therefore ever interesting to all classical readers; but the mighty Amazon excites no other emotions than those which arise from the consideration of its magnitude. Authorities. Condamine, Relation abregée d'un Voy., &c., Acad. des Sciences, Année 1745.—Humboldt, Voyage aux régions équinoxiales.-Poeppig, Reise in Chile, &c. Leipzig, 2 vols., 4to.—Southey's History of Brazil.—Mawe's Journal of a passage from the Pacific to the Atlantic. Lond., 1829.—Smyth's and Lowe's Narrative of a Journey, &c. Lond., 1836, 8vo.—Edwards' Voyage up the Amazon. Lond., 1847 .- Journal of the Royal Geogr. Soc., vols. 2d and 6th.—Bulletin de la Soc. de Geogr., vols. 11th and 18th. AMB

AMAZONS, a supposed fabulous nation of female warriors. An old tradition—which appears to be founded, in some measure, on historical truth—gives an account of a community of women, who permitted no man to reside among them, fought under the conduct of a queen, and constituted a formidable state. They had intercourse with neighbouring nations merely for the sake of preserving their community. The male children they sent back to their fathers; but they brought up the females to war, and burnt off the right breast that this part of the body might not impede them in the use of the bow. From this circumstance, they were called Amazons, i.e., 'Wanting a breast.' The ancients enumerate three nations of Amazons:--lst, the African, who made great conquests under their queen, Myrena; but were afterwards extirpated by Hercules.-2d, The Asiatic, the most famous of all, who dwelt in Cappadocia, on the river Thermodon. These once made war on all Asia, and built Ephesus. Their queen, Hippolyta, was vanquished by Hercules. They attacked Attica in the time of Theseus; and came to the assistance of Troy under their queen, Penthesilea, daughter of Mars and Otrere. About 330 B. c., their queen, Thalestris, made a visit to Alexander of Macedon, soon after which they disappear from history.—3d, The Scythian Amazons, a branch of the Asiatic. They attacked the neighbouring Scythians; but afterwards contracted marriages with them, and made war in company with their husbands.—The old geographers gave the name of Amazonia to a large tract of country in the interior of South America, because the first discoverers of the country said that they found there a nation of Amazons. It is laid down in the old maps as a part of what is at present Brazil and Peru; bounded on the N by Terra Firma and Guayana; on the E by the Atlantic ocean and Brazil; on the S by Paraguay; and on the W by Peru. Even M. de la Condamine brought home many testimonials of the Amazons of the New world, a set of independent women who received men into their society in the month of April only. And M. de Humboldt himself seems inclined to place some credit in the tradition of the existence of a nation of warlike women in South America. His idea is that women, in different parts of that continent, wearied of the degraded state in which they were held, may have occasionally united themselves together as fugitive Negroes sometimes do; and that the desire of preserving their independence may have rendered them warriors.
AMBA, a village in the Punjab, at the base of the Southern Himalaya, in N lat. 31° 40'.

AMBA, an Abyssinian or Ethiopic word, signifying, in the dialects of Amhara and Tigre, a hill, a rock, or a rock-fortress; and of frequent occurrence in the topographical nomenclature of Abyssinia, in which it generally designates a rocky eminence with precipitous sides, rising 300 or 400 ft, above the general level of the surrounding country, which has itself an elevation of from 6,000 to 7,000 ft. above sea-level. Amhara is altogether a succession of fortified ambas or strongholds, the chief of which are Ambucal, Ambadorho, Ambageshé, Ambageshen, Ambalegot, Ambamarjam, Aliuamba, Zosamba, &c.

AMBABICHAI, a small town in Lower Egypt, in the valley of the natron lakes, in N lat. 30° 18', E long. 30° 22'. About 2 m. NE of this town are six lakes of natron, or mineral alkali. The bed of these lakes has a hard and stony bottom, which is dry during nine months of the year. During winter, water of a violet red colour oozes out of the earth, and fills each cavity to the height of five or six feet. The return of the summer's heat evaporates this, and a bed of salt is left, about 2 ft. in thickness, and so very hard that it is broken up with iron bars. About 30,000, quintals are procured from these pits every year. The crystallizations are both of muriate of soda or common salt, and carbonate of soda or natron.

AMBACCA, a regency or district of Congo, forming the most extensive prov. of Angola. Its capital is situated, according to Douville, in S lat. 9° 10', and E long. 17° 37'; and it is bounded on the N by the Mahungos and the Golungo Alto; on the E by the territory of Ginga and Dala Quinçua; on the S by the prov. of Pungo Adongo; and on the W by the Golungo Alto, the Zenza do Golungo, and the Icolo e Bengo. It is divided into eight cantons; and is but thinly peopled.
AMBACKO, a town on the E coast of the island of Celebes, in S lat. 2° 20', E long. 122°.

AMBAGESHEN, a steep rock in Abyssinia, in the prov. of Amhara, in N lat. 10° 50', E long. 39° 10', near the frontiers of Shoa. Almeyda describes it as being "as high above the level plain as a strong arm can sling a stone." It appears to have been used as a state-prison, or a place of safe-keeping for princes of the blood-royal, in former ages.—About 12 m. to the N of it is another natural hill-fortress called Ambaaceb.
AMBAGHAUT, a pass in Hindostan, leading from the Concan into the Western Ghauts in the interior, in N lat. 17° 1', E long. 73° 56'.
AMBAGHUR, a hill-fort in the Deccan, in the prov. of Gundwana, at the base of which lies a small town surrounded by jungle.
AMBAHAI, a mountain of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Samen, on the I. bank of the Taccaze, 70 m. NE of Gondar.
AMBALAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Delhi, 25 m. SE of Sirhind, in N lat. 30° 7'. It is surrounded by walls, and has a good citadel.
AMBALEMA, a town of Bogota, on the Magdalena river, 50 m. W by N of Santa Fe de Bogota.
AMBAMARJAM, a hill-fort of Abyssinia, on the l. bank of the Angrab, SE of Lake Tzana.
AMBAR'ES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Gironde, arrond. of Bourdeaux. Pop. 2,299.
AMBARKIEU, a town of Turkey, in the pashalik of Salonica, on the Gillico river, 13 m. N of Salonica.
AMBATAMB, a town of Madagascar, on a river which flows into the Mozambique channel nearly opposite to Crab isle.
AMBATIKI, a island in the Fiji group in the S Pacific, to the E of Ovolan. It is in shape nearly an equilateral triangle, and rises to an alt. of 750 ft. Pop. 500. - Wilkes.

AMBATO (ASIENTO D'), the capital of a district of the same name in New Grenada. An eruption from the volcano of Cotopaxi destroyed it in 1698, but it has been rebuilt. It is 48 m. SE of Quito, and is situated on a river of the same name, which falls into the Pastaça.

AMBAU, or Ambauo, an island, or rather two islands connected by a coral reef, in the Fiji group, 10 m. to the N of Nukalu. The smaller is entirely covered with houses, and the approaches to it are obstructed by coral reefs; yet it appears to have attained ascendency in the group. The larger island is covered with lofty mountains, and traversed by several rivers.—Belcher.—Wilkes.
AMBAZAC, a town and canton of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, arrond. and 10 m. NNE of Limoges. Pop. 2,822.
AMBELAKE, a village on the strait of Kertche, leading into the sea of Azof, in N lat. 45° 18', E long. 36° 26′.

AMBELAKIA, a town of Greece, in Thessaly, on the r. bank of the Peneus, 13 m. N by E of Larissa. It is "stuck rather than built," on the least wild and rugged part of the declivity of Mount Ossa, above the pass of Tempe. "Nothing can be more picturesque than the various groups of buildings which compose it. Rising out of the thick foliage of woods, overhanging the deep ravines of the mountains, their open galleries and projecting roofs render the effect of situation still more singular and imposing to the eye. The oak, olive, and cypress, spread over the broken surface on which the town stands, and intermix with the foliage of vineyards; while the loftier ridges of the mountain, receding towards the S, are covered with long rows of pines." [Holland.] At the beginning of the present century, it was a place of commercial and manufacturing enterprise, under a special firman of the Porte, which rendered it immediately dependent on the sultan. Its commercial connexions were chiefly with Germany, to which it sent annually about 3,000 bales, of 250 lbs. each, of dyed cottonyarn, the staple commodity of the district, manufactured from cotton grown on the plains of Thessaly. Clarke estimated the number of houses at 7,000; Dr. Holland, at 4,000. But its manufactories, tanneries, college, library, churches, numerous houses, and dense population, disappeared during the revolutionary war. -Beaujour.-Holland.-Urquhart. AMB
AMBELIA, a rocky islet in the gulf of Corinth, half-a-mile from Didascolo.

AMBENAY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Eure, arrond. and 20 m. SW of Evreux, on the Rille. Pop. 902. AMBER, AMBERA, or AMBA RAMBA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Ajmere, 6 m. N of Jypur, in N lat. 27° 2'.—Also a village in Onondago co., New York, U. S., 142 m. NW of Albany.—Also a river of Bavaria, in the Isar circle, flowing into the Isar 2 m. below Mosburg.

AMBER, or Ambre' (Cape), the N extremity of Madagascar, in S lat. 11° 57′ 5″, E long. 49° 13′ 8″. A little to the S of this cape is a low rocky point, formed of hollow cylindrical columns of madrepore, called Point Ambre'. - Owen.
A'MBERA, an isolated peak in Abyssinia, rising from the kolla or low country of Tembien, N 35° W of Samrie, and N 85° W of Abiyad, in N lat. 13° 35', E long. 38° 58'.
AMBERA'N, a large village of Persia, in the district of Velkhi, on the W coast of the Caspian.

AMBERG, a town of Bavaria, in the Regen circle, 32 m. N by W of Ratisbon, and 40 m. E of Nuremberg; in N lat. 49° 25', E long. 11° 50'. Alt. above sea-level 1,250 ft. Pop. 6,000. It is a well-built town, divided into the upper and lower towns by the river Vils, and fortified by a strong double wall, a ditch, and outworks. Its principal manufactures are tobacco, iron, fire-arms, and earthen ware. About 1,400 tons of iron are annually produced from the mines in the vicinity. The archduke Charles defeated the French, under Jourdan, in the neighbourhood of A. on 24th August, 1796.—Also a town of Sweden, in the gov. of Stora Kopparberg, on the r. bank of the Dal, 28 m. WSW of Fahlun.

AMBERGRIS KAY (GREAT and LITTLE), two kays in the Caycos group of the Passage islands, of which the former, in N lat. 21° 19', is 3 m. in length, and about two-thirds of a mile broad, and rises to about 100 ft. in the centre; while the other, to the westward, is low and not distinguishable from the edge of soundings.-Also an island off the coast of Honduras, 27 m. NNE of Balize, about 20 m. in length from NNE to SSW, with an average breadth of 3 m. Its S point is in N lat. 17° 53', W long. 88°.

AMBERIEUX, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Ain, arrond. of Belley.—The cant., which comprises 7 com., had a pop. of 7,900 in 1831.—The com., 22 m. NW of Belley, had a pop. of 2,647 in 1841.
AMBERIEUX-EN-DOMBES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ain, arrond. of Trevoux. Pop. 644.
AMBERLEY, a parish of Sussex, on the Arun river, 4 m. NNE of Arundel. Area 3,030 acres. Pop. 722.
AMBERNAC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Charente, arrond. of Confolens. Pop. 1,087.

AMBERT, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome .- The arrond. has an area of 122,054 hectares, and comprises 8 cant. Pop. in 1831, 87,616; in 1846, 91,734.—The cant., comprising 8 com., had a pop. in 1846 of 7,789. The com. and town, on the Dore, 27 m. E of Issoire, at an alt. of 1,790 ft. above sea-level, had a pop. in 1836 of 8,016. The town carries on an active trade in cheese, which is reputed the best in Auvergne, paper, lace, woollen stuffs, and ribbons, printing, and engraving.

AMBE'S (SAINT-JACQUES D'AMBE'S), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Gironde, arrond. of Bourdeaux, occupying a tongue of land called the Bec d'Ambès, formed by the confluence of the Garonne and the Dordogne. Pop. 792.
AMBIALET, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn, canton of Villefranche, 10 m. E by N of Albi. Pop. 3,623.
AMBIERLE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Loire, arrond. and 9 m. W by N of Roanne. Pop. 1,781.
AMBIE'VILLERS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, arrond. of Lure. Pop. 502.
AMBIORBY, a town of Sweden, on the l. bank of the Klar, 71 m. N of Carlstadt.
AMBLAU, one of the Molucca islands, 6 m. S by E of Bouro, in N lat. 3° 53', E long. 127° 2'. It is about 15 m. in circumference; and is regarded by the Dutch as a dependency of Amboyna.
AMBLECOAT, a hamlet in the parish of Old Swinford, Staffordshire, about half-a-mile N of Stourbridge. Pop. 1,623.

AMBLESIDE, a chapelry and town of Westmoreland, on the upper part of Windermere, 14 m. NW of Kendal. Pop. 1,281. Woollen cloth is extensively manufactured here for the Kendal market; and slate is quarried in the neighbourhood; but the chief importance of the town is derived from its picturesque attractions, and the number of tourists who annually visit the surrounding lake-district. The town overlooks a valley about 3 m. in length, at the upper extremity of which is Fairfield, a mountain rising to an alt. of 2,950 ft. above sea-level; and at the other, Windermere stretches its calm expanse.
AMBLESTON, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 8 m. N by E of Haverford West. Pop. 605.

AMBLETEUSE, a small port of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, arrond. and 5 m. N of Boulogne, at the mouth of the little river Selaque, in N lat. 50° 48'. Pop. 595. James II. landed here after his abdication, on 5th January, 1689.

AMBLEVE, a river rising in the Prussian prov. of the Lower Rhine, near Heppenbach, and after passing Malmedy, entering Belgium, where it passes Stavelot and Nonceveux, and joins the Ourte on the r. bank, at Dousoflamme, 10 m. S by E of Liege, after a course of about 50 m.
AMBLEVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, arrond. of Cognac. Pop. 426.
AMBO, a village of Peru, situated in the angle formed by the confluence of the Huecar with the Huallaga. Pop. betwixt 400 and 500.
AMBOINA. See AMBOYNA.

AMBOISE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, arrond. of Tours.—The cant., comprising 15 com., had in 1831 a pop. of 14,541.—The pop. of the com. in 1841 was 4,613, of whom the greater part formed the pop. of the town, which is on the l. bank of the Loire, 14 m. E of Tours, and 127 SW of Paris. The citadel or castle, which is of great extent, is built on a rock about 90 ft. in height above the river, around which, and on an island in the river, the town is built. A suburb on the opposite side of the river is connected with it by a wooden bridge. The manufacture of files, steel implements, and fire-arms, is the chief trade. The town is surrounded by vineyards, and the whole district is remarkable for its rich cultivation. Louis Philippe modernized the castle, which is connected with many historical recollections. Here Louis XI., in 1469, instituted the order of St. Michael; here his successor, Charles VIII., was born in 1470, and here also he died in 1498, and here, in 1560, the duke of Guise took bloody vengeance upon the Huguenots for the Conjuration d'Amboise, as it was called.

AMBOISES, three islets in the bight of Biafra, between the NE extremity of Fernando Po and in the woods. The latter are the only aborigines of AMB the main. The largest, MONDOLEH, is only half-amile in length. It presents a level surface of the richest soil on decomposed basalt. The outer island is called Dameh or Ambas. It is a narrow ridge of rock, but has a pop. of from 300 to 400. The third islet is called Bobia.

AMBOJAR, the first watering-place in the Desert, on the route from Cosseir to the Nile. Here the road is shut in on both sides by walls of rock, except where little plains of sand, dotted with large stones, extend. In general this part of the Desert may be described as consisting of vast masses of irregular rock, of all shapes, and varying in height from 10 to 100 ft.; in some places frowning from the slanting sides of larger cliffs; in others just piercing the sand with their thin knife-like edge; in others clustered mass on mass in the wildest disorder; and in others arranging themselves into long avenues or lanes; while here and there small spots ranging from an area of 100 square yards to a square mile, appear free of rocks and stones, and covered with sand, or with the little yellow thorny acacia.
AMBOLON, one of the Philippine islands, to the S of Mindoro, in N lat. 12° 10', E long. 121° 10'.
AMBON, a commune and port of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, arrond. of Vannes, on the Penerf. Pop. 2,175. It exports salt.
AMBONG, a town on the NW coast of Borneo, at the mouth of a river falling into a deep bay of the same name, in N lat. 6° 14'.
AMBOY, a township in Oswego co. New York, U. S., 80 m. E of Oswego, on the Salmon and Scriba creeks. Pop. 1,070.—Also a town in Lucas co. Ohio. Pop. 450.

AMBOYNA, or Amboina, [Malay, Ambun,] an island of the Indian archipelago, in the Molucca group, situated to the SW of the island of Ceram; 120 m. E of Batavia. It consists of two unequal parts, distinguished by the names of Leytimor and Hitu, formed by two indentations of the coast, and connected by a narrow isthmus called Baguala. Binnen bay, the larger of these, entering the SW coast, is upwards of 21 m. in length. Its entrance, which is 7 m. in breadth, is between two lofty points rising abruptly from the sea, but in some places its width does not exceed 1 m. The Dutch attempted to cut a passage through the isthmus, and in part succeeded; but this cut has been partially filled up; large prahus, however, are said to be floated or carried across it. [Belcher.] The extreme length of the island from NE to SW is estimated at 32 m., and its average breadth at about 10 m. Both sections are bristled with wooded mountains, and watered by numerous rills, which in the rainy season swell into destructive torrents. The soil—except in the vallies, in which it is more arenaceous—consists of a reddish clay; and is generally highly fertile, producing, besides all the fruits and vegetables common to Tropical regions, coffee and indigo, the latter of an excellent description, but in small quantities. Among the other vegetable productions are the clove-tree, the sago-tree—the farinaceous pith of which constitutes the principal article of subsistence of the Amboynese, a single tree of 18 inches diam. affording subsistence to a family for a month or six weeks-the cajeputtree, the teak-tree, in small quantities, the ebony and iron-tree, the casuarina, the wild clove, the nutmeg, the samarna tree, the nain-tree, of which the Chinese make anchors and rudders, and many other varieties of valuable wood, amounting in number to about 400. Lingoa, or Amboyna wood, as it is called, is mostly produced in the island of Ceram. Sheep and oxen are scarce, but horses, buffaloes, goats, and swine, are numerous; and deer and wild hogs abound the domestic species of quadrupeds in the island; and their flesh constitutes the only animal food used by the inhabitants. A. possesses no beasts of prey; but it is infested by about 20 species of snakes. [Valentyn.] Poultry is abundant; and the sea-coasts afford large quantities of fish. Gold in considerable quantities is found in the streams; the surfaces of the mountains are incrusted with sulphur; and asbestos of excellent quality and potter's clay are found in various parts of the island.—The staple commerce of A. consists in the produce of its clove-plantations, estimated at an annual amount of 1,000,000 lbs. It is entirely in the hands of the Dutch, who guard the monopoly, not only of the trade, but of the culture of the clove, with the most illiberal jealousy. The clovetree (Eugenia caryophyllata) attains maturity in from 9 to 15 years, and continues to bear for about 50. It resembles the pear-tree in form; its branches spread, its leaves are long and pointed like the laurel, and it grows from 20 to 40 ft. high. The cloves, which are at first little dark green longish buds, become yellow, afterwards red, and are gathered in October and November. The average crop of each tree is from 4 to 6 lbs. It is only in A. and the three adjacent islands of Harakau, Saparona, and Nussa Laut, that cloves are now cultivated.—The climate of A. is generally warm and dry. Stavorinus found the mean heat during three months at Amboyna from 80° to 82°; the greatest was 91°; the least 72°. The monsoons are regular in their periodical recurrence, but are exactly the reverse of those of Java and the other islands to the E of the meridian of the straits of Salayer; so that while Lombok, Java, and Borneo are deluged with rain, the weather is here, and throughout the Moluccas, clear and dry. The E monsoon is here rainy and tempestuous; the W, dry and moderate. The tides and currents are inconstant and unequal; the former rising from 6 to 9 ft., and sometimes only once in the twenty-four hours. The aborigines of A. belong to the savage tribe of the Alforas or Haraforas, and a few remnants of them are still to be found in the recesses of the forests. The Malays, who now constitute the predominant population, although audacious in disposition, are yet indolent and effeminate in their habits. They have adopted the general style of the Dutch dress. They are fond of music and festive entertainments; and are skilful in the management of their canoes, which are from 10 to 20 ft. long. Their war-boats, named korokores, vary in length from 80 to 100 ft.; and are capable of receiving 60 or 100 men. They manufacture various ornamental articles, as work-boxes, urns, prahus, &c., from the clove. The houses are constructed of branches of the palm-tree; and on account of the frequency of earthquakes, generally consist of only one story. Although the only legalized foreign settlers, the number of Chinese resident in A. is small compared with the proportion which they form in the population of the other islands of the Asiatic archipelago; and the Europeans constitute a still smaller proportion of its inhabitants.—This island is the residence of the governor of the Moluccas, and is under the subordinate jurisdiction of rajahs of three distinct classes. The Chinese are superintended by an officer of their own nation. Mahommedanism was introduced to Amboyna in 1815, contemporaneous with the discovery of the island, and is still the prevailing religon; although, after being taken possession of by the Portuguese in 1564, zealous efforts were made by that nation to proselytize the inhabitants to their faith. In 1605, the Portuguese were here supplanted by the Dutch. A factory was established by the English at Cambello in 1614; but was destroyed by the Dutch, under circumstances of atrocious injustice, in 1622. In 1796 A. was cap- CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CO AMB tured by the English, but by the peace of Amiens, in 1801, it was given back to Holland; in 1810 it was retaken by the English, but was restored to the Dutch by the treaty of Paris, four years afterwards. The total pop. was estimated by Cannabich in 1837 at 45,000; when the English took the island in 1796, it was estimated at 45,252, of whom 17,813 were Protestants. The language most commonly spoken by the Amboynese is the Malay. Amboyna, the chief town of the above island, and next to Batavia the most important of the Dutch possessions in the Asiatic archipelago, is situated in N lat. 3° 41', E long. 128° 15'; on the E side of Binnen bay, towards the centre of the coast of the western and smaller section of the island. It is a neat, well-built town, and is well-supplied with water. The streets are straight, broad, and well-paved. The houses, about 1,000 in number, are constructed of wood, and covered with palm leaves. In its canals, bridges, and public buildings, A. has quite the aspect of a Dutch town. It contains 2 Christian churches, one belonging to the Europeans, the other to the Malays; a town-house, consisting of two stories, and situated opposite the esplanade which leads to the fort; an hospital, a fine public garden, and a menagerie. The bazaars or market-places are very handsome. At the southern extremity of the esplanade, is a handsome range of houses, -surrounded by double rows of nutmeg trees, -in which the principal inhabitants reside. Fort Victoria, which contains the government-offices, is in the form of a parallelogram, extending angularly towards the sea, and defended on the land side by a ditch. It is garrisoned by Dutch and Malay troops; but is of little avail as a fortress, being commanded by adjacent heights. The principal defence of the island consists in the inaccessible character of its coasts. The best anchorage is abreast the town, in from 20 to 35 fath. The pop. of the town of Amboyna is about 7,000. According to M. Duperrey, the variation of the needle here, in October 1823, was 0° 28′ 3″ E; mean dip 20° 32′ 3″. -Stavorinus's Voyages, in Wilcocke's Translation, vol. 2d.—Captain Belcher's Voyage, vol. 2d.—Milburn's Oriental Commerce. - Crawfurd's Indian Archipelago.
AMBRA, a village of Tuscany, in the prov. of Arezzo, 5 m. S of Bucine, on a rivulet of the same name which flows into the Arno on the l. bank. Pop. 577.
AMBRACIA. See ARTA.
AMBRADDY, a river of Hindostan, in the prov. of Coimbatur, flowing into the Cavary, 8 m. ENE of Carur.
AMBRAKIA, a village of Greece, 24 m. E of Vonitza, on the gulf of Arta.
AMBRAS. See Innsbruck.
AMBRES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Tarn, arrond. of Lavaur. Pop. 1,440.
AMBRI, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Ticino, on the Ticino river, 6 m. NW of Faido.

AMBRIE'RES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Mayenne.—The cant., comprising 8 com., had a pop. of 11,430 in 1831.—The com. and t., on the Mayenne, 6 m. N of Mayenne, had a pop. of 2,399 in 1841.

AMBRIZ, a small state on the coast of Lower Guinea, of which the cap., called Quibanzo, is about 9 leagues inland from the town and bay which gives name to the state. It is governed by an absolutely despotie king, who must, however, be elected every five years from among the mafuks or principal officers of state. The Portuguese and others trading with A. obtain gum-copal, elephants' and hippopotamus' teeth, and slaves. The soil is fertile; and tobacco, manioc, maize, and beans are cultivated. Toms, who visited the cap. of Ambriz, describes the ground as in many places literally covered with flowers, or where these were wanting, with noble trees; while flights of humming-birds, and richly coloured butterflies, filled the air.—The town of A. is in S lat. 7° 52', E long. 13° 3', on the S side of a bay of the same name, and the l. bank of a small river, which, after watering an extensive and fertile valley, flows into this bay. A vast quantity of gum, especially copal, is brought to market here.
AMBRO. See AMBER.

AMBROGIO (SAN), a commune and village of Sardinia, near the Doire, 13 m. W by N of Turin. Pop. 1,200. There is a celebrated abbey here.— Also a village of Lombardy, in the district of Verona, 642. in the vicinity of which are hot springs.

AMBROIX (SAINT), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Gard, arrond. of Alais. -The cant., comprising 16 com., had a pop. of 13,973 in 1831.—The com. and v. are 14 m. NE of Alais. Pop. 2,947. A considerable quantity of silk is raised in the neighbourhood.—Also a com. in the dep. of Cher, arrond. of Bourges.
AMBRONAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, arrond. of Belley. A celebrated abbey of Ben edictines formerly existed here.
AMBROOK, an island of Russia, off the coast of Livonia, 5 leagues ENE from the S point of Oesel.
AMBROSE (SAINT), or AMBROSIA, an island off the coast of Chili, in S lat. 26° 30', W long. 79° 38', 4 leagues W of St. Felix isle.
AMBROSEDEN, a parish and village in Oxfordshire. Area of p. 5,210 acres. Pop. 892. The v. is 2½ m. SE by S of Bicester.
AMBROSETOWN, a parish in co. Wexford, 6 m. S by W of Taghmon. Area 2,197 acres. Pop. 690.

AMBRYN, an island of the New Hebrides, in about S lat. 16° 18', E long. 155° 55'. It was discovered by Bougainville in 1768. Cook sighted it in 1774. It appears to be about 50 m. in circuit. The shores are low, but the centre of the island is occupied by mountains, in which an active volcanic crater occurs. The SW coast slopes into a fine plain, adorned with stately timber. The soil is fertile, and the island appears to be well-inhabited.

AMBUKOL, a village in Kordofan, to the SE of Old Dongola, about a mile from the Nile. The cultivated land extends from the v. to the river, but only a small portion is tilled; and the Desert has here encroached Georgia. much on the N bank of the river. The principal articles of commerce here are dhourra, cattle, sheep, goats, asses, butter, grease, and cotton, the latter of excellent quality. About 8 m. W of A. is a curious sandy waste, called Haagbarlak, the superficial stratum of which, a coarse sandstone, contains many siliceous fossil trees. The Desert between A. and El Hajir, called the desert of Bayudeh, was crossed by Consul Holroyd in a 52 hours' march from NW to SE, which, at 21 m. per hour, would give a distance of 130 m. From El Hajir to Khartum the distance was 16 hours 20 min., or about 42 miles.
AMBULE, a river and district of Madagascar, on the E coast. The district is fertile, and is reported to be rich in iron.

AMBULI, or AMBOWLEE, a village in the island of Salsetta, on the W coast of Hindostan, celebrated for the cave-temple in its vicinity, which is similar in plan to that of Elephanta, but inferior in extent; and being excavated in stone of a soft porous character, is fast crumbling into decay.

AMBULUM, an extensive plain between Tripoli and Sockna, which is entered by a pass to the southward of Benioleed. Its surface is in many places destitute of vegetation, with here and there rocky AME eminences, and patches of fine gravel mixed with shells .- Oudney.
AMBUN, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 30 m. W of Arcot. The chief employment of the inhabitants is rearing the castor-oil plant, and preparing the oil for exportation.
AMDAWARK, a narrow mountain-ridge between the Mizrib and the Arri, a tributary of the Takkazie, in the district of Lasta in Abyssinia.—Beke.
AMDORP, a village of Hanover, in East Friesland, on the Leda, near its junction with the Jumme, 6 m. W of Detern.
